title: Untitled
provenance_text:
Formerly in the Collection of Zainab and Ruxana Pathan.
This early watercolour by Krishnaji Howlaji Ara offers a rare glimpse into the artist's largely undocumented pre-Progressive practice. Painted during a politically charged period, the work reflects Ara's direct engagement with India's struggle for independence. In 1930, at just sixteen, he joined Gandhi's Salt Satyagraha, one of the most influential acts of civil disobedience, and was subsequently imprisoned for five months. As Yashodhara Dalmia notes, 'since life on the edge was an essential driving force with Ara, it was inevitable that he would be drawn into the surging atmosphere of the Independence movement. His own participation in the freedom movement gave him a view of the rising crescendo of the struggle from the inside.' (Yashodhara Dalmia, 'The Stillness of Life: Krishnaji Howlaji Ara', The Making of Modern Indian Art: The Progressives, New Delhi, 2001, p. 137) Created three years later, the painting belongs to a moment when Ara's experiences with anti-colonial activism were shaping both his worldview and the ideological foundations of his early artistic production. Through its mythological subject matter and revivalist visual language, the work transcends aesthetic experimentation, reflecting the ethical and political atmosphere in which Ara was forging his identity as both a citizen and an artist under colonial rule.
This idyllic scene from the Ramayana, set within a forest hermitage, captures the lives of Lord Ram's sons, Luv and Kush, during the years they spent in the forest at the poet sage Valmiki's ashram. The twin boys lived with the sage, and were brought up by him and Sita. Here, Ara captures an important part of their education - learning to use a bow and arrow - one brother prepares to release an arrow under the careful guidance of Valmiki, who gently steadies his arm. title: Untitled
exhibited_text:
Ram Kumar, Y.M.C.A., Simla, 1949.
'I was excited when 'Caravan of India' suggested to hold my solo exhibition in Simla. It was the summer of 1948. Simla was full of tourists. General Cariappa opened my exhibition. I had hoped to sell him a painting but when he left just after shaking hands with me, I was annoyed. Some days later, I ran into Dr. Zakir Husain on the ridge. He was in Simla in connection with some UG meeting. He had been my teacher in Delhi University. I went upto him and introduced myself and invited him to my exhibition. The next day he came. Not only he took keen interest in my show, he also asked me questions which pleasantly flattered me. At the end of it when he expressed his desire to buy 4 of my works, I couldn't believe it. My first ever paintings were being sold and that too, to a person who was held in great esteem by everybody.' (Ram Kumar, 'From Ram Kumar's Notebooks - Paintings on a Bullock-cart', Ram Kumar A Journey Within, New Delhi, 1996, p. 195)
The two gouache works on paper in the current sale (lots 15 and 17) represent two of the earliest works by Ram Kumar to come up at auction. Both works come from the family of Dr. Zakir Husain, an educator and professor who went on to become India's third President. The young Ram Kumar had first met Dr. Husain while he was a student at St. Stephen's College in New Delhi in the late 1930s. He quite enjoyed the academic setting which provided him with the perfect environment to develop his Hindi short-story writing skills. It was only in the early 1940s that he began painting, quite by chance, when he enrolled in the Sarada Ukil Art School in Connaught Circle, to better understand the tenets of Western painting. Ram Kumar spent the better part of the decade alternating between conventional jobs, such as working in a bank for his uncle in Simla, or developing both his writing and painting skills, which had a mutually beneficial effect on each other. title: Untitled
literature_text:
Husain, exhibition catalogue, Kumar Art Gallery, New Delhi, 1959, unpaginated, illustrated.
In many of M.F. Husain's gouaches from the early 1950s, women are the central characters. They can be depicted as part of a family group, or gathered together in mutual support of one another, or even occasionally seated in complete isolation. Whatever the final choice of composition, they are created in a fusion of styles that is as much influenced by European modes of expression, as it is Indian folk art.
As Geeta Kapur states, there was a meeting between the artist's 'deeply entrenched Indian sensibility with the art of Europe, particularly France and Italy... However, both the raw vigour of his earliest paintings and the taut drama of the 'folk' phase are mellowed. Here they melt into the contours of the woman, gentle but vital and illuminated by an impressionistic articulation of paint. She may be likened to Modigliani's women because of the shape of her limbs; indeed this may be a conscious assimilation. However, the essential spirit is Husain's own, very traceably linked to his attitude toward the woman in all his paintings. He paints her over and over, not for her languorous sensuousness, but for her warmth of humanness, that appears sometimes through her exposed fecundity, sometimes through the sadness of eyes, sometimes as here in her young and live vulnerability.' (Geeta Kapur, Husain, Sadanga Series for Vakils, Mumbai, p. 39)
The characters that appear in these early paintings develop both conceptually and in painterly terms over the course of the following decade.  During this period, the female figures of Husain's canvases increasingly appear to represent an artistic response to the trauma that the artist experienced at the loss of his own mother at a young age. They are frequently veiled or faceless, their identity intentionally hidden in shadow, reflective of the emotional void left by the absent maternal figure in his life. exhibited_text: Husain, Kumar Art Gallery, New Delhi, 29 November - 7 December, 1959.

title: Untitled
literature_text:
Anne Macklin, S H Raza: Catalogue Raisonné,1972 - 1989 (Volume II), New Delhi, 2022, p. 374, illustrated.
Raza, Vakil & Sons Ltd., Mumbai, 1985, unpaginated, illustrated.
SH Raza: The Journey of a Master, New Delhi, 2014, p. 113, illustrated.
S.H. Raza's early career in India during the 1940s was defined by expressionistic landscapes and cityscapes, primarily rendered in watercolours and gouache. After moving to Mumbai (then Bombay) in the early 1940s, he co-founded the revolutionary Bombay Progressive Artists' Group in 1947, with his contemporaries F.N. Souza, M.F. Husain and K. H. Ara. This group aimed to break away from the influences of both British academic realism and the revivalist Bengal School, seeking instead a modern vernacular that integrated an Indian 'inner vision' with international avant-garde styles. In July 1948, the PAG presented an exhibition at the Artists' Salon on Rampart Row which was inaugurated by Dr. Mulk Raj Anand. Raza recalls, 'it created a lot of interest and controversy with people for and against our work. But we didn't care one way or the other and we were happy that a dialogue had been initiated, that in our association, we were helpful to each other, that in our meetings, exchanging of books and ideas, there was a tremendous enthusiasm and a burning desire to work. And the one aspect that was the most important thing at the time was a sense of freedom and experimentation in what we were doing. It didn't matter if things went wrong. What was important was to try and see what would happen.' (Yashodhara Dalmia, Journeys, Four Generations of Indian Artists in Their Own Words, vol. 1, New Delhi, 2011, p. 49)
Despite the PAG's initial successes and the general enthusiasm surrounding their work, Raza became dissatisfied with his situation. He understood that to evolve as an artist he had to move on. 'I was acclaimed because the standards at that time were not very high. But we were in the dark. I did not know what art was. I did not know
exhibited_text: Raza, Gallery Chemould, Mumbai, February 1984.

title: Untitled
provenance_text:
Acquired directly from the artist by the current owner.
Throughout his career, Benaras remained of critical importance to Ram Kumar. From his earliest visit in 1959, when he visited the Eternal City along with his friend M.F. Husain, the city, with its ancient history and decaying architecture, made an immediate and lasting impression on him that resulted in the creation of a significant body of works at periodic intervals from the 1960s through the 1990s. He revisited the city on several occasions, and would often wander around sketching various sights and scenes, which were then developed into larger compositions on canvas.
In the early 1980s, after a break of just over a decade, the distinct cityscape of Benaras re-appears in Ram Kumar's paintings. The earlier works from the 1960s used thick built-up paint layers in darker tones of green, blue and brown to capture the crowded river-bank with its mystical energy and underbelly of suffering, often interpreting the city through the artist's own reflections rather than a realistic cityscape. In contrast, for this group of works, he chose a far lighter monochromatic palette, with an emphasis on white, concentrating far more on the architectural features of the buildings, with doorways, staircases, arches and roofs all jostling for space along the river-bank. The only visual respite comes in the foreground of the paintings, which are comparatively free of man-made structures, allowing space and a symbolic pause for the Ganges flowing by.
Around the same time, in the early 1980s, M.F. Husain had been busy experimenting with a new medium in New Delhi. Having met the then Development Commissioner, Handlooms, Mr. S.K. Mishra, along with Mr. Amrut Patel from the Weavers' Service Centre, Husain had been very excited at the prospect of creating woven tapestries of selected images from his oeuvre. Shortly after this fortuitous meeting, he and Mr. Patel worked at the Weavers' Service Centre in New Delhi and created six tapestri

title: Untitled
medium_raw: Remembering Bhupen - An Exhibition of Oils & Watercolours of Bhupen Khakhar, Garden Art Gallery, Mumbai 11-19 March, 2005.
provenance_text: Formerly in the Collection of Chester and Davida Herwitz.
exhibited_text:
Remembering Bhupen - An Exhibition of Oils & Watercolours of Bhupen Khakhar, Garden Art Gallery, Mumbai 11-19 March, 2005.
The Master of Mischief, Paintings by Bhupen Khakhar
‘So it came to me just like that: it arrived in an email, a jpeg of a painting I had never before seen, called In a Boat. And, if normally I don’t write about jpegs, in most cases that is, here I am tempted. I will go ahead. For I do see this: its brooding intensity, an intensity of darkness and almost over-strained luminosities that hold me and seduce me, tame and frame me in their sombre glare; I know also that I can see Keith Vaughan and John Craxton, over- heated, over-outed, drift across my screen, and that the heat of St Ives can never generate these tones, nor the Cornish coast this vastness of feeling. The painter has transformed their phenomenology of being-gay, saturated the colours of its flaming into public life in the generations that followed on from them. This jpeg has, I think, a structure, a pictorial structure of many knowledges and ways of feeling.’ (Adrian Rifkin, ‘In a Boat’, Bhupen Khakhar You Can’t Please All, exhibition catalogue, Tate Modern, London, 2016, p. 58)
This short extract, written by Adrian Rifkin in Bhupen Khakhar’s retrospective exhibition catalogue, identifies many key elements of the artist’s work, but it is the phrase ‘a pictorial structure of many knowledges and ways of feeling’ that is most insightful, for Bhupen’s paintings and career represents a fine balancing act between multiple seemingly contradictory elements, within his own life and personality. He was a professional accountant yet a self-taught artist, he was a writer of short stories, whose friends included artists and critics, yet one of his best friends was an illiterate bootlegger. His painting style is termed naïve, yet it was informed and influenced by many diverse traditions. He was gay, but often joked about having a wife and children. title: Untitled
provenance_text: Acquired by the current owner from Vadehra Art Gallery, New Delhi in 1999.
literature_text:
Chaitanya Sambrani, Ed., At Home in the World - The Art and Life of Gulam Mohammed Sheikh, New Delhi, 2019, p. 205, illustrated.
Kahat Kabir Gouaches by Gulammohammed Sheikh, exhibition catalogue, Vadhera Art Gallery, New Delhi, 1998, unpaginated, illustrated.
Gulammohammed Sheikh's Kahat Kabir or Thus Spoke Kabir series are a group of twenty works painted between 1986 and 2002. The paintings explore the artist's profound fascination with the poetry and teachings of Kabir, the 15th century poet and saint. Executed in watercolour and gouache, they combine the artist's ethereal use of colour with delicate drawings of mystics and the landscape. Kabir appears frequently over the course of Sheikh's seven decades of painting, as the artist feels a particular affinity between his images and the poet's words. Moreover, in a world overrun by violence and communal strife, he looks to Kabir to be a guiding inspiration of peace.
No one knows the secret of the weaver
Who spread his warp through the universe
He dug two ditches, sky and earth,
Made two spools, sun and moon,
Filled his shuttle with a thousand threads
And weaves till today: a difficult length!
Kabir says, they're joined by actions.
Good threads and bad,
That fellow weaves both.
(Kabir, Ramaini 28, The Bijak of Kabir, translated by Linda Hess and Sudhdev Singh, San Francisco, 1983, p. 84, reprinted in At Home in the World - The Art and Life of Gulam Mohammed Sheikh, New Delhi, 2019, p. 139)
exhibited_text:
Of Worlds Within Worlds - Gulam Mohammed Sheikh - A Retrospective, Kiran Nadar Museum of Art, New Delhi, 6 February - 30 June, 2025.
Kahat Kabir Gouaches by Gulammohammed Sheikh, Vadhera Art Gallery, New Delhi, 25 December, 1998 - 5 January, 1999.

'At the back of every conversation with Arpita one senses something unspoken, something elusive and mysterious below the surface of a simple exchange of words. The world of the child and the primitive world of folk painting meet in her world of magic. But there is one difference: Arpita is neither a child nor a villager. She is a young woman painter who has studied the techniques of painting and is married to a painter; her friends are painters and sculptors. She has chosen to express herself largely through the medium of painting and so her work is conscious and sophisticated rather than naïve. It is the subtle balance she maintains between the consciousness of what she is doing and the unconscious act of painting that provides the dynamics of her role as woman, as painter, and consequently of the viewer who participate in her work.' (Pria Karunakar, 'Arpita Singh', Lalit Kala Contemporary 15, New Delhi, February 1973, p. 25)
The current work was created as part of an ideological retreat for artists organised by Manjit Bawa in Dalhousie in 1991. Inspired by fellow artist Vivan Sundaram's artist programs at the Kasauli Art Centre that had begun in the mid-1970s, Manjit was keen to re-create a similar stimulating atmosphere of thinking and creativity with artists that shared a common philosophy. These included Rameshwar Broota, Vasundhara Tewari, Paramjit Singh, and of course, Arpita Singh. He and Arpita shared a close relationship, as a result of which he often appears in her compositions.

The current work is a colourful and sweeping aerial view of Chowpatty from a vantage point up on Malabar Hill. The repeated forms of the crowded buildings are broken by the cheerful, uplifting palette Raza has used for the rooftops and the facades. The elegant curve of Marine Drive and the enclosed beach and bay are captured on a brilliantly bright day; sunshine bathing the entire scene in clear sparkling light. Sailboats float peacefully on the calm water, and groups of people mill around the streets, presenting an idyllic weekend scene.
EMANUEL SCHLESINGER AND S.H. RAZA
'Walter and Käthe Langhammer, Rudolf and Albrecht von Leyden and Emanuel Schlesinger were essential in promoting an avant-garde art movement in Bombay: the Progressive Artists Group. Together with Indian visionaries, these Austrian and German refugees from National Socialism initiated new ways of evaluating, generating and looking at fine art. Besides creating a new audience through the media and sustainability through collecting and other forms of financial help, they consolidated the movement by generating exhibition spaces from private venues to public galleries... they were catalysts in the development of post-colonial Indian art.' (Margit Franz, 'From Dinner Parties to Galleries: The Langhammer-Leyden-Schlesinger Circle in Bombay 1940s through the 1950s', Arrival Cities: Migrating Artists and New Metropolitan Topographies in the 20th Century, Leuven, 2020, p. 73)
A group of works painted by Sayed Haider Raza and other artists from the Collection of Emanuel Schlesinger included in the current sale encapsulate one of the most exciting and important periods of cross-cultural camaraderie in modern Indian art history. title: Untitled
literature_text:
Richard Bartholomew & Shiv S. Kapur, Husain, New York, 1969, pl. 19, illustrated.

Diamond Jubilee Exhibition, exhibition catalogue, Bombay Art Society, Mumbai, 1948, p. 20, illustrated.
'Husain's art originates from his tactile contact with life and brings to it an interpretive insight, recognising the moment when the subject reveals its truth. He establishes a relationship with the movement of life, yet his art is not of the moment, it is not ephemeral. The dynamic and the elemental forces are held in tension within a captured moment. He selects images that hold essences and can be made to enact a persistent visual drama in the picture space. Husain creates human characters that confront his emotive ideas; to reach further into the terrains of the human condition he introduces with them, a dialogue of metaphorical characters.' (Geeta Kapur, Husain, Sadanga Series for Vakils, Mumbai, p. 3)
In 1935, at the age of twenty-two, M. F. Husain moved to Mumbai.  At this nascent stage in his career, he lived on a stretch of pavement outside Peela House. After a short while, he moved to Badar Bagh, a Bohra enclave near the red-light district of Falkland Road. It was here that he met his wife, Fazila Bibi, and where his children were born. While living there, he supported his family by painting billboards and cinema banners under streetlights, taking advantage of the brief intervals at night when the trams were stopped. During the day, he would walk through the streets of Lohar Chawl, offering to paint portraits of traders and their families for the modest sum of 10 rupees per portrait to supplement his meagre income. From this, the humblest of beginnings, he was to emerge a few decades later as the most celebrated Indian artist of his generation.
The current work was created around the same time F.N. Souza had asked Hu

literature_text:

'Studies in the Development of Three Contemporaries', Marg, Mumbai, vol. VII, no. 2, March 1954, unpaginated, illustrated.
Mohan Samant was born in Mumbai, and studied art at the Sir J.J. School of Art, before joining the Progressive Artists Group in 1952, and ultimately spending most of his career working in New York. He joined the PAG on the invitation of K.H. Ara, after founding members F.N. Souza and S.H. Raza had already left India to pursue opportunities in Europe. Despite the constant change of members, the group met with remarkable regularity at the home of Krishen Khanna and organised three key shows during these early years. The 1953 Progressive Artists Group show featured the work of eight members including Samant. During these early exhibitions, he found a loyal and keen patron in Emanuel Schlesinger who became an avid collector of his works, and often visited him at his studio in Goregaon.
Samant's early works from the 1940s and 1950s tended to incorporate abstraction and figuration both, looking to the Cubists and Abstract Expressionists, including Paul Klee, Pablo Picasso and Willem de Kooning. Equally, he was inspired by his art school teacher, Shankar Palsikar, both in shared images and influences, looking back to Indian miniature painting traditions for both composition and subjects. He was also fascinated with ancient temples and sacred monuments coupled with Hindu mythology and classical Indian sculpture. Along with Indian culture, ancient Egyptian art and architecture also contributed to the styles and subje

title: Untitled
literature_text:
Suhas Bahulkar, The Bombay Art Society (1888-2016) History and Voyage Vol. I, exhibition catalogue, National Gallery of Modern Art, Mumbai, 2017, p. 207, illustrated.
'Even in his earliest work a quality of mysticism was evident. His lurid colours-the flaming reds, the absorbent violets and ultramarines, and the unflinching greens in his painting Sleepless Night - are shot with an apocalyptical vision. There is a Rouault-like incandescence in Samant's work. Colour is employed emotively, and most of Samant's paintings vibrate. One word best describes these paintings-incantation.' (Richard Bartholomew, 'Contemporary Indian Painting', Modern Art of Asia, 1961, reprinted in Richard Bartholomew, The Art Critic, New Delhi, 2012, p. 70)
This painting, with its thick black outlines and bold forms captures Samant's quest to create a personal style that was uniquely his own. Although it does have Cubist overtures, the rich palette combined with the distinctly defined features of the female figure in the left half, and the geometric rendering of the building, possibly a church, in the right half, defy a conventional definition. The two subjects co-exist seamlessly on the canvas through a cohesive use of colour and brushstrokes.
exhibited_text: The Bombay Art Society (1888-2016) History and Voyage, National Gallery of Modern Art, Mumbai, 10 February - 26 March, 2017.

title: Untitled
provenance_text:
Acquired directly from the artist.
Born in Indore in 1910, Narayan Shridhar Bendre was known for his prolific artistic practice that included landscape, portrait and still-life paintings, rendered in a variety of styles and techniques that evolved and changed over his long career. In 1941, Bendre won the Bombay Art Society Gold Medal for his watercolour titled Benaras Landscape. In 1943, he was hailed by The Times of India as the leading artist of his generation. To his further artistic credit, his vision became a driving force for other artists that were to follow in his wake. For a brief period, he mentored M.F. Husain, and, likewise, encouraged K.H. Ara to continue with his vocation as an artist. In 1950, he was appointed as the Head of the Department of Painting at the Faculty of Fine Arts at M.S. University in Baroda and his work as a teacher had a profound influence on an entire generation of Baroda based artists, who studied under him and treated him like a guru. In retrospect, he must be considered one of the most influential Indian artists of the twentieth century.
Throughout his long career, Bendre travelled extensively. In 1947, he visited the United States, and on his return journey to India, he travelled through London, Amsterdam and Paris. During this extended period of travel, he was introduced to Euro-American avant-garde movements like Cubism, Impressionism and Expressionism. In 1952, he went to China, as part of a cultural delegation at the invitation of Jawaharlal Nehru, where he studied Chinese art. In 1958, he went on an extended trip to the Middle East, where he undertook a similar survey of the architecture and art forms of the region, finding striking similarities to that of ancient India.
It was in 1957, between his trips to China and the Middle East that Bendre completed the current painting. The arcadian scene appears to depict an Indian tribal family in a tender moment of relaxation. The muscular crouching male holds a long bow in his rig

title: Untitled
provenance_text:
Gallery Chemould, Mumbai.
Sagara's geometric inclinations are apparent in the form of the current lot, a painting that depicts a muted array of angular and structured houses seemingly stacked upon one another. He has created a work that is both flat yet textured, still yet dynamic; his layering technique, through the use of cross-hatching and scraping, offers a tactile quality to the piece that both deepens the illusion of age and wear. The orientation of the homes feel ambiguous; they tilt, press, and lean into each other, disrupting the viewers sense of spatial logic. The repetition of modular forms lends the work an almost musical cadence, serving as a visual fugue of urban living. This painting showcases his ability to blend architectural rigidity with a lyrical abstraction each structure is meticulously composed and simultaneously subtly distorted, suggesting a sense of compression, fragmentation, and memory.

title: Untitled

Sao Paulo Biennale, Sao Paulo, 1971.
As a young boy in Indore, Maqbool would accompany his friend Mankeshwar to watch the Ram Lila. Part of the Ramayana, the Lila was a dramatic folk re-enactment of the life of Lord Ram, which ended in a battle between Lord Ram and the evil king Ravana. Husain's father would contribute money to the performances held in his neighbourhood. Even though the young Husain could not understand all the aspects of the play, the sounds and visuals captivated him. It is important to note that even at this early stage in his life, that Husain, despite being a devout Muslim, was surrounded by the influences of a multi-cultural India.
In 1968, the freedom fighter and socialist leader, Dr. Ram Monahar Lohia commented on the limited reach of modern art to the Indian population. The majority of Indians that lived in villages had no understanding or context to the art of their own time. His suggestion was for Husain to paint scenes from the Ramayana, a subject they were familiar with, and exhibit these paintings in the villages, as a way of bridging the past with the present. The idea immediately appealed to Husain, and he began research by listening to recitations of Tulsidas' Ramayana by local pandits at the home of Badrivishal Pitti in Hyderabad.
Childhood memories of the Ram Lila combined with these new explanations and interpretations conjured fresh images for Husain. Once a preliminary body of work was ready, Husain toured the paintings on a convoy of bullock carts through the surrounding villages. Spontaneous exhibitions were set up in several village squares along the route. Husain was amazed and humbled by the response. His viewers were fascinated and could easily understand and relate to the characters, despite their modernist treatment by the artist. He was not faced with a barrage of questions on why and how the characters were represented, as was frequently the case with his urban audience.

Jehangir Art Gallery Inaugural Exhibition 61 Annual Exhibition, exhibition catalogue, Bombay Art Society, Mumbai, 1952, p. 31, Illustrated.
Jehangir Sabavala was born in 1922 into a prominent Parsi family in Mumbai (then Bombay). He began his early education at Fishers Institute in Territet, Switzerland, and later attended the Cathedral and John Connon School in Mumbai. During the war years, he stayed in the city, initially studying English Literature at Elphinstone College before transferring to the Sir J. J. School of Art. In 1945, he moved to London to study at the Heatherley School of Fine Art, and subsequently continued his training in Paris from 1947 to 1951.
While in Paris, Sabavala moved between Académie Julian and the Académie André Lhote, and later, the Académie de la Grande Chaumière. It was his time with Lhote, the ‘academician of cubism’, that proved to be especially formative. ‘Through him, Sabavala internalised the radical cubist doctrines of conception and perception: the Cubist emphasis on regarding the perceived object, as constituted, not by its essence, but by its relation to other objects. Underscoring conceptual process rather than retinal impression, the cubist painting demonstrated its subject as an assemblage of planes – as a structure autonomous of nature, the product of sometimes contradictory stresses and devices rather than a harmonious unity of effects.’ (Ranjit Hoskote, Pilgrim, Exile, Sorcerer, The Painterly Evolution of Jehangir Sabavala, Mumbai, 1998, p. 49)
Upon his return to Mumbai in 1951, the artist held an exhibition of the works he had produced during his time in Europe, which focused on Western Academic subjects including still lifes and portraits. In technique as well, these early works were largely influenced by the impressionist idiom of colour and brushstroke, resulting in graceful forms and a
exhibited_text: Jehangir Art Gallery Inaugural Exhibition 61 Annual Exhibition, Bombay Art Society, Mumbai, February 1952.

title: Untitled
provenance_text:
Artists Centre, Mumbai, 2001.
Born in 1914 in Bolarum, Secunderabad, Krishnaji Howlaji Ara faced hardship early in life. His mother died when he was just three years old, and life grew more difficult after his father’s remarriage. At the age of seven, he ran away to Mumbai (then Bombay), where he eventually found work as a domestic helper.
When Ara’s employer discovered his talent for painting, she supported his artistic pursuits by providing him with watercolours and allowing him a few hours off each day to practice drawing and painting objects. In 1930, he joined Gandhi’s Salt Satyagraha and was imprisoned for five months. Upon his release, a friend who worked as a chauffeur for a Japanese businessman from the Yokohama Corporation helped him find work washing cars for 18 rupees a month. ‘Luckily for Ara, his talent didn’t go unnoticed. His employers recognised his flair for painting and let him use his spare time to paint at the garage where he lived and worked. They even purchased his artworks… and encouraged him to submit his drawings to exhibitions.’ (Reema Desai Gehi, ‘Don’t Mix With These Fellows’, The Catalyst: Rudolf Von Leyden and India’s Artistic Awakening, New Delhi, 2024, p. 75) Following the bombing of Pearl Harbour, the Japanese businessman fled Mumbai, leaving Ara in charge of the property. He soon settled into the staff quarters at Sital Baug in Walkeshwar, which became his lifelong studio.
Unemployed at the time, Ara was supported by Rudy von Leyden, art critic for The Times of India, who covered his expenses and offered him a modest stipend, enabling him to dedicate himself to painting full-time. In 1942, he held his first solo exhibition at Chetana restaurant on Rampart Row. Five years later, on the eve of Independence in 1947, he was one of the founding members of the Progressive Artists’ Group along with F.N. Souza, S.H. Raza, M.F. Husain, H.A. Gade, and S.K. Bakre.

literature_text:
Jasmine Shah Varma, ‘Collector’, Art India The Art News Magazine of India, vol. 8, issue 4, Mumbai, 2003, pp. 92 - 93, illustrated.
‘The glimpse of truth in one’s work is more important to me than whether you use colour well or other such technical virtuosity. Truth and… a certain kind of confession. In our situation, there are hardly any other painters touching these sexual subjects. I guess people only want to hide. Toh usmey maja nahin aata hai [There’s no fun in that].’ (Artist statement, ‘Sadanand Menon interviews Bhupen Khakhar’, Bhupen Khakhar: You Can’t Please All, exhibition catalogue, Tate Modern, London, 2003, p. 166)
Bhupen Khakhar was an artist, playwright, and poet whose vivid narratives collapsed the distance between the banal and the mythical, the personal and the political. Born in Mumbai (then Bombay) to a middle-class Gujarati family in 1934, Khakhar grew up the youngest of four children and was the first in his family to pursue a college education. Trained as a chartered accountant, he dutifully shouldered his family’s career aspirations; art remained an idle passion, relegated to unsatisfying evening classes at the Sir J.J. School of Art. It wasn’t until his move to Baroda in 1962, and his enrollment at the Faculty of Fine Arts, that Khakhar began to engage fully with his creative potential.
Unlike many of his contemporaries, Khakhar’s artistic journey began in earnest in his thirties—late, by conventional standards—but it was perhaps this belated arrival that lent his practices its more introspective and unflinching qualities. His first medium was the experimental collage, the works composed of cutouts from popular prints and advertisements whose style would later earn him the moniker of “India’s first Pop artist”. Nevertheless, this work ultimately felt limiting to Khakhar. ‘I felt it was too restrictive. If I wanted to draw a face, I couldn’t. It was too much of a game.’ (Artist statement, Timothy Hyman, ‘The Baroda Convergence (1964-72)’, Bh

auction_title: Mf Husain An Artists Vision Of The Xx Century
title: Acrylic on canvas
literature_text:
P. Bhargav, Paintings by M.F. Husain, Mumbai, 2004, unpaginated, illustrated. P. Bhargav, Vision XX Century: Paintings by M. F. Husain, Mumbai, 2004, unpaginated, illustrated.
K. Bikram Singh, Maqbool Fida Husain, New Delhi, 2007, p. 200, illustrated.
With its expressionist approach to human, animal, natural and architectural forms, this painting brings together the robust vitality of the poster and the stylised forms of calligraphy. In a scene reminiscent of West Asian romances such as ‘Leili and Kais’ (Laila and Majnun’) or ‘Shirin-Farhad’, both featuring a pair of star-crossed lovers, a veiled woman sets out on a journey on camel-back from an oasis, presumably away from her loved one. These narratives have continued to animate fabular lineages of storytelling, as well as theatre and cinema, to the present day; their presence in Husain’s universal history of the 20th century is enthralling. We see how, also, despite his strong humanist emphasis, Husain remained sensitive to the fragile beauty of the natural world: to the plant and animal kingdoms, as well as to desert and coastal topographies distant from the metropolis that was his preferred habitat. Drawing on an established paradigm in West Asian culture, he bears manifest witness here to the dyad of the desert and the garden, the arid wasteland and the well-watered sanctuary. [RH]
exhibited_text:
M. F. Husain, Pierre Cardin Centre, Paris, November 2004.
M. F. Husain: An Artist’s Vision of the XX Century, Burj Al Arab, Dubai, 16 June 2004.

auction_title: Mf Husain An Artists Vision Of The Xx Century
title: Acrylic on canvas
literature_text:
P. Bhargav, Paintings by M.F. Husain, Mumbai, 2004, unpaginated, illustrated.
P. Bhargav, Vision XX Century: Paintings by M. F. Husain, Mumbai, 2004, unpaginated, illustrated.
In this painting, Husain pays homage to the fortitude of India’s poverty-stricken millions, many of them obliged to migrate from the neglected rural hinterland to the flourishing metropolitan centres, there to be conscripted into the labour force. The point of view in this painting is striking. We appear to be looking down at a pair of pavement dwellers from above, past the dark rim of a street lamp that casts bright light on a seated figure, while another figure finds refuge in sleep. An alms bowl symbolises their tenuous means of livelihood. Husain had lived through several momentous political and economic transitions in India – from colonial extractivist rule to a nascent nation-state experimenting with a command economy to the free-market liberalisation of the early 1990s. He retained a keen eye for economic shifts and the effects they could unleash. His own journey from the artisanate to the affluent classes never robbed him of his empathy for those less fortunate than himself. He always remained deeply in sympathy with the poor, the vulnerable and the marginalised at the edges of society. [RH]
exhibited_text: M. F. Husain: An Artist’s Vision of the XX Century, Burj Al Arab, Dubai, 16 June 2004.

‘The Hiss and Crackle of the Line’
Excerpts from a conversation between Sudhir Patwardhan (SP) and Ranjit Hoskote (RH)
‘RH: I am very interested in the comparative architecture, if you will, of your paintings and your drawings. Your paintings, typically, are meticulously planned, with rival perspectives, alternative vistas and asymmetries of scale folded into one another to create a riddling and allusive multiplicity for the eye, and no easy anchorage. By contrast, your drawings are more direct, clear, and sometimes even declarative. Your drawings register more spontaneous engagements with the world's surprises and upheavals, or more immediate statements of the self's discontents and ambiguities. Would you comment?
SP: Yes, that is very true again. Drawing and painting have been like two poles, counterpoints of my activity as an artist. The natural movement of the activity of drawing for me is towards expressive catharsis. To get into a state where all mediation melts away. And the natural movement of my painting is towards clear, rational, absolute truth. Of course, I can get to neither end, as the other pole is always pulling. So I try and get more of my drawing into my painting – which translates as becoming more painterly!
The apparent contradiction here may be due to thinking of drawing as linear and therefore clear, and painting as fluid and messy. But I am thinking of painting as clear, sharp and meticulously layered, and drawing as aggressive, neurotic. There seem to be more contradictions lurking here! …

‘Nandalal was steadfast in his pursuit of art which was his truth, un-deterred by criticism and un-lured by tempting offers. Great as an artist, he was greater as a teacher, and greater still as a man. That is why he could win the admiration and affection of both poet Tagore and Mahatma Gandhi.’ (Krishna Kripalani, Nandalal: The Man and the Artist, Visva Bharati News, Nandalal Bose, Centenary Number, ed. Ananth Nath Das and Bijaya Bhattacharya, Santiniketan, 1982, p. 36)
An important aspect of this middle period of Nandalal's career was his remarkable association with Mahatma Gandhi, which began at the Indore annual session of Congress in 1934, took more concrete forms in the 1936 and 1937 Congress sessions at Lucknow and Faizpur, and culminated finally in the 1938 session at Haripura. At the opening of the Faizpur session, Gandhi said, 'Credit for the arrangements here belongs to the architect Mhatre and the artist Nandalal Bose. When Nanda Babu responded to my invitation a couple of months ago I explained what I wanted and left it to him to give concrete shape to the conception. For he is a creative artist and I am none. God has given me the sense of art, but not the organs to give it concrete shape. He has blessed Nandalal Bose with both.' (Collected Works of Mahatma Gandhi, digital edition, vol. 70, p. 212)
In February 1938, Gandhi placed Nandalal Bose in charge of creating a unique temporary township decorated with local art for the Indian National Congress convention at Haripura. Nandalal visited the site and made numerous notes and plans for the design of the space on small format postcards. Gateways, halls, and huts were created for the conference ground, made out of thatch and bamboo according to his designs, and they were decorated with earthenware pots, baskets and textiles, all made by local craftsmen. literature_text:
Debdutta Gupta, Postcards by Nandalal Bose, exhibition catalogue, Akar Prakar, Kolkata, 2011, unpaginated, illustrated.
The current lot is an original illustration created by Nandalal Bose, to a children’s play, Tak Duma Dum written by Jnanadanandini Tagore, who was married to Satyendranath Tagore, Rabindranath Tagore’s eldest brother. The author is best known as a social reformer who influenced the earliest phase of women’s empowerment in 19th Century Bengal, but she also participated in the rich literary traditions of the Tagore family, writing articles for the Bengali journal Bharati. In 1885, Jnanadanandini Devi established Balak, the first children’s literary magazine in Bengali to which Rabindranath contributed a number of poems, plays and short stories. Jnanadanandini also wrote for the publication, including two plays, Tak Duma Dum and Sat Bhai Champa both of which were highly regarded in literary circles at the time.
The story of Tak Duma Dum revolves around a jackal who gets thorns stuck in his whiskers when trying to steal aubergines from the village barber’s vegetable patch. The jackal approaches the barber to ask for his assistance to get the thorns out. The barber uses his tweezers to help, but he hurts the jackal. As compensation for his discomfort, the barber gives the jackal the tweezers. Later, the jackal sees a potter in his garden trying to dig his garden with his bare hands. The jackal allows him to borrow his tweezers, which break, so the potter offers him a pot in compensation for his loss. Moving on, the jackal meets a gardener trying to water his plants with his hands, so he offers him the use of his pot, but again the gardener breaks the pot. In exchange for the broken pot, he gives the jackal a Bengali groom’s wedding hat, called a Topor. Later that day, he spots a wedding procession with a groom en route to fetch his bride, but the groom has no hat, so the jackal loans the groom his hat. provenance_text:
The current lot was acquired by Fritz Schleicher in 1903, when he purchased the Ravi Varma Fine Arts Lithographic Press and its contents from the artist Raja Ravi Varma.
Raja Ravi Varma was born Ravi Varma Koil Thampuran in Kilimanoor village, in the erstwhile state of Travancore in South India. The family of Kilimanoor, to which he belonged, enjoyed particularly close ties with the Travancore royal family, thereby allowing him to have a close relationship with the ruling royal family.  Following traditional norms, Ravi Varma was educated in the Kilimanoor Palace. However, in addition to the regular curriculum, his uncle, sensing a special talent, added a special class of drawing for the young boy, ...'even at the tender age of five or six, he would cram the walls of his home with pictures of animals and scenes from daily life done with chalk and charcoal, despite the scoldings he regularly got for 'spoiling' the walls.' (Parsram Mangharam, Raja Ravi Varma The Painter Prince 1848 - 1906, Bangalore, 2003, p. 17)
Ravi Varma grew up in an environment rich with the arts. The Royal family of Travancore had always been active patrons of the arts, including music, painting and sculpture, and his proximity to the family ensured that he was exposed to these forms from a young age. His talent as a draughtsman was spotted at the age of thirteen by the Maharaja, who had been presented with drawings by Ravi Varma, and was impressed with what he saw. He instructed the boy to live in the palace and learn from the other artists working in the court at the time. The Indian court artists had recently been introduced to two novel concepts: canvas and oil paint, both alien to the artists of South India, but critical elements to the European academic style that was favoured at the time. As they were still learning how to make optimal use of these magical mediums, there was a fair amount of caution and jealousy that existed amongst the artists. None were willing to be generous with their

provenance_text: The current lot was acquired by Fritz Schleicher in 1903, when he purchased the Ravi Varma Fine Arts Lithographic Press and its contents from the artist Raja Ravi Varma.
literature_text:
Rupika Chawla, Raja Ravi Varma, Painter of Colonial India, Ahmedabad, 2010, p. 138, illustrated.
When Raja Ravi Varma’s primary patron and supporter, the Maharaja of Travancore, passed away in 1880, the artist began travelling around the country more frequently, accepting invitations from various royal families, including Baroda, Pune, and Mysore. Due in part to the fraudulent activities of his business partner, Govardhandas Khatau Makhanji, and the disruption to his printing press that had been caused by the plague, his financial situation remained uncertain and he continued to look for new commissions and patrons. His partner on his travels was his brother, Raja Raja Varma, who was an artist as well as a writer. His focus remained portraiture and mythological scenes, which coincided with the subject choice of his new royal patrons.
One influential figure, for whom Ravi Varma had not yet worked, was Mahbub Ali Khan Asaf Jah VI, the Nizam of Hyderabad, who ruled the state between 1869 and 1911. The Nizam was known for his extravagant lifestyle, in particular, his love of cars, clothes and jewellery. It was rumoured that he had devoted an entire wing of his palace to his wardrobe, and certainly his collection of clothes was one of the most extensive anywhere in the world. Clearly an artistic commission from such a wealthy and influential figure would have been immensely beneficial to the Varma brothers, but they did not have personal connections at the court.
However, in 1902, the court photographer, Raja Deen Dayal, invited the Varma brothers to Hyderabad with the promise that he would try to arrange them an audience with the Nizam. On 22 January, 1902 Raja Raja writes, ‘At noon we started with Dharma Chandra for Hyderabad; visited on the way Bashir Bagh the residence of the late minister, a splendid mansion. Next drove to the Char Minar and to the present minister’s quarters which are old fashioned.’ A day later he writes, ‘we commenced a fancy picture to be shown

title: Untitled
literature_text:
This work will be included in the forthcoming SH Raza, Catalogue Raisonné by Anne Macklin.
‘Raza justifies his preference for water colour on the plea that it is cheaper and lends itself to easier outdoor painting. Reasons apart, he has translated his partiality for water colours into some specimens truly representative of his reactions to the outside world. depending more on colour as a vehicle of expression, he splashes on paper in a carefree manner and succeeds in producing the correct atmosphere, as well as the innermost feeling and emotions of the subjects he handles.
He creates forms not by outlining colours but by contrasting them. His works always have a refreshing air about them, which arrests the immediate attentions of observers. In his quick wash work he just drops colour of the figures and lifts them out or throws them into the background and produces a sense of space. With a bold brush, he is predominantly impressionist; his colours are vital but his anatomy is weak. He has a great future before him in the field of art in this country.
We miss the linear qualities in his painting, but the impressions are always striking. There is an unfinished appearance in his works, but that only stirs the imagination of the observer. He is an artist of profundity.’
(Manu Thacker, ‘Raza: Landscape Artist, Painters of the Present’, The Illustrated Weekly of India, 11 July 1948, reprinted in Manu Thacker and G Venkatachalam, Present-day Painters of India, Mumbai, 1950, pp. 39 – 41.)

literature_text:
Manasji Majumder, Close to Events, Works of Bikash Bhattacharjee, New Delhi, 2007, p. 31, illustrated.
Making realism the core of his practice at a time when it was considered a regressive trend, Bikash Bhattacharjee set himself apart from his contemporaries. His technical mastery over the style gave him the ability to imbue his paintings with complex, multi-layered meanings through deliberate distortions. His illusionary realities juxtapose the real with the unreal, creating haunting and hypnotic imagery infused with a transcendental glow. The present lot, a surrealist pastiche, is an unsettling amalgamation of fragmented faces and muted gestures, and comments on the ways in which personal and collective histories intersect at the most primal levels.
Surrealism, a movement that flourished in Europe between the two world wars, was a radical revolt against the constraints of reason and reality. At its core, the movement sought to transcend conventional thought and explore the irrational, delving into the unconscious mind. This movement emerged in the wake of WWI, a time when traditional values and beliefs had been shattered by the horrors of war. Surrealism rejected the linear, logical order that had governed art and society, and instead, embraced the chaotic, irrational, and often nonsensical elements of dreams, fantasies, and automatic processes, thereby allowing artists to access hidden layers of the human psyche. Through absurdity and juxtaposition, surrealists attacked the bourgeoisie order, questioning the systems of power, authority, and consumerism. The devastation left a profound mark on the collective psyche, and surrealists sought to give form to the emotional and psychological wounds left in its wake.

literature_text:
Citing the city, Sudhir Patwardhan, exhibition catalogue, Sakshi Gallery, Mumbai, 2007-2008, p. 27, illustrated.
Sudhir Patwardhan, a retired radiologist and self-taught artist, began his professional painting career in the 1970s after relocating to Mumbai (then Bombay) from Pune. A chronicler of the urban and social landscape of Mumbai, Patwardhan is a keen observer of the world around him— he pays attention to the working class, to the interactions between individuals and infrastructure, and to the shifting dynamics of people around him, and beyond.
In parallel with his development of complex, large-format oil canvases, often depicting the urban landscape and the ordinary man, he also refined his figurative style through drawing. ‘The format of drawing allowed Patwardhan to keep pace with the rapid pulse-rate of the city and also address complex questions without too much pre-meditation, which wasn't possible in the more deliberated format of painting. The drawings operate as autonomous art-works; they are rendered across an array of formats and surfaces, switching technique and medium each time as if to discourage their being viewed as linked, linear narratives.’ (Jitish Kallat, ‘Citing the City: Recent Drawings and Paintings by Sudhir Patwardhan’, Citing the city: Sudhir Patwardhan, exhibition catalogue, Sakshi Gallery, Mumbai, 2007, p. 9)
The current sale includes four drawings by Patwardhan lots 33-36, three of which are figurative. These works span four decades, from the 1970s to the 2000s, with one work from each decade. Patwardhan’s pre-occupation with the human figure dates back to 1969, when he was pursuing an MBBS at the Armed Forces Medical College in Pune. Through the readings of Durant, Sartre and Camus, Patwardhan grew interested in the how individuals dealt with the universal problem of being human. ‘I was not interested in actual physical deformities. But yes, expression, posture, grimace, and also distortions were a sign of this struggle to be hum
exhibited_text: Citing the city, Sudhir Patwardhan, Sakshi Gallery, Mumbai, November 2007 - March 2008.

‘Souza has given to art a great deal more than he has taken from it. His painting is intensely personal, to the point of being esoteric. To appreciate it, one has to participate in certain preoccupations and fears which make his visual distortions explicable and sympathetic. This is a gesture which the spectator has to be prepared to make with all ‘expressionist’ art, from Grünewald and Bosch to Munch and Kokoschka. And Souza’s distortions lend themselves to rational explanation no more than do those of Bosch and Kokoschka. If he was creating monsters, probably no one would be troubled; but because his images are clearly intended to be human, one is compelled to ask why his faces have eyes high up in the forehead, or else scattered in profusion all over the face; why he paints mouths that stretch like hair combs across the face, and limbs that branch out like thistles. Souza’s imagery is not a surrealist vision – a self-conscious aesthetic shock – so much as a spontaneous re-creation of the world as he has seen it, distilled in the mind by a host of private experiences and associations.’ (Edwin Mullins, Souza, London, 1962, p. 39)
Similar to his relationship with the Church, Souza shared a complex equation with women, both in his personal life and in the various avatars that would be thrust upon his female protagonists. He was influenced by numerous female figures over the course of his life, ranging from his mother, to his wives and lovers, to his daughters. His connection with so many women gave him a nuanced understanding of just how precarious a balance needed to be maintained between thoughts, actions and emotions. ‘For Souza, relationships with women were multifaceted. He was supported and nurtured by them on an emotional and financial level. He relished in their physical beauty and the oneness of intimate union. He valued that in their multiplicity, they became his window into multiplicity. In essence, what Souza received fr

During his address to the ‘Modern Art in India Symposium’ of 1960, Souza stated ‘we did have an ancient heritage, a great culture and a great civilisation, which alas, we can no longer inherit, because between the ancient and the contemporary there is too wide a breach, an unbridged gorge, a hiatus. The invaders missionaries, conquistadors, and Victorians, castrated our aesthetic potentialities, or we just dwindled on our own and became culturally impotent. In this crater remain ignorance, intolerance, philistinism, vulgarity and bad taste, alien imitations, ... rusty cannon balls, brass buttons, helmets, and remains of old boots and dead cats.’ (F.N. Souza, ‘A Personal Statement, Modern Art in India Symposium’, Illustrated Weekly of India, New Delhi, 17 July 1960, p. 55.)
The polemical nature of this statement is characteristic of the writings and speeches of F.N. Souza. Never one to sugar coat his commentary, Souza condemns everyone but himself for the lamentable state of contemporary art in the country. His statement is at once a call to arms to reclaim the lost vitality of ancient Indian art traditions, and yet, an admission, that the moment may have passed. His early paintings dating from the late 1940s through to the late 1950s are, in part, his own contemporary response to India’s ancient culture. This is particularly apparent when one considers the sources of inspiration that he chooses for his female nude figures which frequently drew upon Hindu and Buddhist classical sculptural traditions.
‘A look at Souza’s female forms is illustrative of Classical Indian art which remained in the shadows for too long. Breaking a long tradition since the Greek Classical era and totally abandoning the Western tradition of the Capitoline Venus which represents the modest Venus, Souza painted women in the image of the sculptures of Khajuraho and Yogini temples of India. They are also reminiscent of the Yakshis of Sanchi. Showing remarkable

‘Though they are mostly open-mouthed, the faces wear expressions of exhaustion, gaiety, surprise, a death-like ghastliness. They gesticulate and grasp at things. Their voices reverberate, though the reverberation is in a measure of colour. There are mute colours and shrill colours and colours that denote quiet and peace, of the dead and of the living. As these figures live out their lives in paint, their past and future are lumped together in the premise of paint. They are linked inexorably to this locale. They are strangely naked yet completely dressed, for it is feeling and not flesh that is revealed. And this feeling is not of the flesh satisfied or strained but of the spirit, which is timeless.’ (Richard Bartholomew, ‘Twelve Compositions with the Figure by Tyeb Mehta’, Thought, 19 February, 1966, reprinted in Richard Bartholomew, The Art Critic, New Delhi, 2012, p. 414)
Right from his early years as a painter, Tyeb Mehta had always been preoccupied with the human condition. While his technique and stylistic choices may have evolved from the early period to his more mature years, his interest in human beings and their suffering has remained consistent. He himself faced several difficulties in his early years, which influenced his choice of subjects as he identified with them quite closely.
‘The human figure has become part of my vocabulary, like a certain way of applying colour or breaking up images. It is a sort of vehicle for me. I am not a minimalist or abstract painter... my work is still expressionist. The human figure is my source, what I primarily react to ... I put a certain distance between myself as the seer and the canvas as the seen to allow the painting to exist as an entity in its own right.’ (Tyeb Mehta in conversation with Nikki Ty-Tomkins Seth, Tyeb Mehta: Ideas

Aside from the more provocative renditions of women in Souza’s art (see lots 10 and 13), he also spent a considerable amount of his career painting the more conventional subjects of landscapes and still lifes. All three categories would remain integral to his art throughout the five decades of his career, with a steady evolution and change distinctly visible in the style and technique employed over the course of his life. He was a thinker and a writer, and he expressed himself through both words and art. As his thinking evolved, so did his approach to painting. Geeta Kapur identifies this change in artistic language as the artist ‘devising an appropriate language of forms.’ (Geeta Kapur, Contemporary Indian Artists, New Delhi, 1979, p. 33) For Kapur, the question is whether Souza could develop appropriate artistic languages for his changing subjects, but in fact, of greater concern, to the artist himself, was whether he could devise a painterly process that reflected his understanding of the more universal creative process of Nature itself. The point is perhaps one and the same, for it is in the depiction of Nature, in his landscapes, and in the vibrant flower filled vases of his still life paintings, that we confront Souza’s evolution at its earliest stage of his painterly style.
The Still Life as a genre is a primarily Western construct, having emerged in Dutch painting in the 17th century. It remained a favourite subject for artists all around Europe through the Impressionists and the Cubists, all the way through to Fernand Leger in the early 20th century. Souza’s still life paintings were distinctly different from their predecessors. They did not have any ‘bourgeois’ leanings, nor did they refer to the consumer culture that had overtaken the West post World War II. His Still Life paintings of the 1950s were markedly different from the works of the 1960s, which took on a frenzied energy and vibrancy that was missing from the mor

‘The ‘subject’ instead of imposing itself as an obvious visible reality or experience, gradually transformed into a painted entity, alive, airy, breathing. Living life fully and starting with himself, he [Bal Chhabda] has followed his own incentives, gone in one single direction, with slow spaced rhythm, to reach his own destination: within.' (S.H. Raza, ‘Bal Chhabda 1923 - 2013’, Mumbai Modern: Progressive Artists’ Group 1947 - 2013, exhibition catalogue, Delhi Art Gallery, New Delhi, 2013, p. 353)
Filmmaker turned artist, Bal Chhabda began his career in Hollywood working in film production. After a year-long stint working on Mr Blandings Builds his Dream House and Enchantment starring Cary Grant and Myrna Loy, and David Niven respectively, Chhabda decided to return to India in 1950 to write and co-direct a screenplay, Do Raha (1952), starring Nalini Jaywant and Shekhar. The film delved into the conflict between art and love, focusing on a woman who sacrificed her lover in pursuit of art. Inspired by M.F. Husain’s 1946-47 artworks, Chhabda integrated them into the film. On Kekoo Gandhy’s recommendation, he then enlisted S.K. Bakre alongside M.F. Husain to collaborate on designing the film’s sets. Chhabda and Husain, who would later become lifelong friends, parted ways for some years when the film failed at the box office.
During a chance meeting between the two in 1956, Husain invited him to visit the Bhulabhai Desai Institute at Warden Road. There, he encountered artists Tyeb Mehta, S.H. Raza, Krishen Khanna, Ram Kumar and V.S. Gaitonde. ‘He watched their early bursts of endeavour, interested, at times fascinated. Quite by accident, on the spur of a compelling moment, he sketched a crooner, sketched some more. And one day, borrowing canvas and paint, painted his first oil, directly,’ thus becoming a honorary member of the Progressive Artists’ Group. ‘I never wanted to paint, it never even occurred to me. Warli Painting - A Family Affair
Originally from the people living in the Thane district of Maharashtra, situated along the foothills of the Sahyadri mountains, Warli paintings are rooted in an ancient artistic tradition practiced by the Warli community, an indigenous, adivasi tribe. These artworks adorn the walls of huts across the villages and are painted by savāśinīs, village artists with living husbands. Adhering to a consistent style, they typically feature a central figure, the mother goddess Pālaghata, surrounded by an intricately decorated chaukat, set against a backdrop of towering trees where animals and humans co-exist. Created in anticipation of weddings, these paintings, known as lagnāche chitra, play a significant role in the marriage ritual, and are considered indispensable for its successful culmination.
Beyond their ritual function, the Warli people view these paintings as potent expressions of their worldview. Historically, Warli paintings were solely created by women, using white rice paint on walls plastered with mud and cow dung. However, in the 1970s, the Government introduced a programme to encourage Warli artists to adapt their craft for the urban market by painting on paper, a mobile medium.
The pioneering artist who took this art form from mud walls to paper and canvas was Jivya Soma Mashe. ‘Mashe entered this domain of women’s art, at the early age of three or four, when he was abandoned by his father and stepmother. He remembers the pleasures of the wedding season when he would get plenty of festive food to eat, often in return for rendering assistance to women painters engaged in ritual wall painting for the occasion. Mashe helped the women by grinding raw rice to prepare the white pigment, bringing water for them, washing the grinding stone and holding the cup of rice paste for them. While doing so, it seemed he entered the enchanted world of Warli painting, got initiated into its vocabulary and mastered its te

Of the great European artists working in India in the 18th and 19th centuries, it was undoubtedly the Daniells, Thomas and his nephew William, who played a pre-eminent role in recording and documenting the country for European eyes. The aquatints of India by the Daniells have been continuously popular ever since their publication between 1795 and 1810. The British serving in India purchased them for their libraries or framed them for their houses, offices and clubs. In the early 19th century, collectors eagerly acquired them as a celebration of the ‘sublime’, the ‘picturesque’ and the exotic as well as to record many of the recently documented heritage sites of India.
Other artists, notably William Hodges (1744–1797), who made a tour of the Ganges in 1780 - 1783, provided inspiration for the Daniells, and in 1786 they set off from England to make their fortune in India. In the six years they spent in India, they ventured further than any previous European artists, completing three tours around the country; along the Ganges from Kolkata (then Calcutta) to Srinagar, 1788 - 1791, a tour from Chennai (then Madras), 1792 - 1793, and finally, before their return to England in 1793, a tour of the temple sites in and around Mumbai (then Bombay). The current lot depicts a view of the Krishnagiri Fort, painted during their South India tour.
In the spring of 1792, the Daniells left Calcutta by boat for Madras, and they spent the rest of that year in the south of the sub-continent. The area had been made topical for British audiences by the recent string of Mysore wars. William Hodges had been prevented from exploring the interior because of the outbreak of the second Mysore War in 1780, but the Daniells were more fortunate, since their arrival coincided with the end of the third war. These protracted hostilities between the British and the Sultans of Mysore were not to be fully re

The current lot was acquired by Fritz Schleicher in 1903, when he purchased the Ravi Varma Fine Arts Lithographic Press and its contents from the artist Raja Ravi Varma.
Raja Ravi Varma and the Fine Arts Lithographic Press
Raja Ravi Varma was born Ravi Varma Koil Thampuran in Kilimanoor village, in the erstwhile state of Travancore in South India. The family of Kilimanoor, to which he belonged, had very close ties with the Travancore royal family, thereby allowing him to have a close relationship with them. Following traditional norms, Ravi Varma was educated in the Kilimanoor Palace. However, in addition to the regular curriculum, his uncle, sensing a special talent, added a special class of drawing for the young boy, ‘...even at the tender age of five or six, he would cram the walls of his home with pictures of animals and scenes from daily life done with chalk and charcoal, despite the scoldings he regularly got for ‘spoiling’ the walls.’ (Parsram Mangharam, Raja Ravi Varma The Painter Prince 1848 - 1906, Bangalore, 2003, p. 17)
Ravi Varma grew up in an environment rich with the arts. The Royal family of Travancore had always been active patrons of the arts, including music, painting and sculpture, and his proximity to the family ensured that he was exposed to these forms from a young age. His skill as a draughtsman was spotted at the age of thirteen by the Maharaja, who had been presented with drawings by Ravi Varma, and was thoroughly impressed. He instructed the boy to live in the palace and learn from the other artists working in the court at the time. The Indian court artists had recently been introduced to two novel concepts: canvas and oil paint, both alien to the artists of South India, but critical elements to the European academic style that was currently in favour. As they were still learning how to make optimal use of these magical mediums, there was a fair amount of caution and jealousy that existed amongst the artists. None were willing to be genero

Debdutta Gupta, Postcards by Nandalal Bose, exhibition catalogue, Akar Prakar, Kolkata, 2011, unpaginated, illustrated.
The following two lots are original illustrations to a children’s play, Tak Duma Dum, written by Jnanadanandini Tagore, who was married to Satyendranath Tagore, Rabindranath Tagore’s eldest brother. The author is best known as a social reformer, who influenced the earliest phase of women’s empowerment in 19th Century Bengal, and she also participated in the rich literary traditions of the Tagore family, writing articles for the Bengali journal Bharati. In 1885, Jnanadanandini Devi established Balak, the first children’s literary magazine in Bengali, to which Rabindranath contributed a number of poems, plays, and short stories. Jnanadanandini also wrote for the publication; notably, two plays, Tak Duma Dum and Sat Bhai Champa, both of which were highly regarded in literary circles at the time.
Tak Duma Dum revolves around a jackal who gets thorns stuck in his whiskers when trying to steal aubergines from the village barber’s vegetable patch. The jackal approaches the barber to ask for his assistance in getting the thorns out, but as the barber uses his tweezers, he hurts the jackal. As compensation for his discomfort, the barber gives the jackal the tweezers. Later, the jackal sees a potter in his garden trying to dig up the soil with his bare hands. The jackal, in an attempt to help, offers him his newly acquired tweezers, which promptly break, and the potter offers him a pot as compensation for his loss. Moving on, the jackal meets a gardener trying to water his plants with his hands, so yet again, to be helpful, he offers him the use of his pot, but alas, the gardener breaks the pot. In exchange for the broken pot, he gives the jackal a Bengali groom’s wedding hat called a Topor. Later that day, the jackal spots a wedding procession led by a groom going to fetch his bride, but the groom has no hat, so the jackal loans the groom his hat. The follo

Images in silhouette were a popular style of book illustration in England during the late 19th century, also referred to as the Golden Age of British Book Illustration. Illustrators such as Arthur Rackham (1867–1939) (image in the catalogue), often used this technique of black figures against a pale background. It seems that Nandalal Bose has experimented with and cleverly adopted a similar style for a few of his own illustrations to this popular Bengali tale, with unique and interesting results.

‘Should Davierwalla stop working at this point, and we know he will not, he has rendered to modern sculpture the service of two or three artists. It may be the poverty of contemporary sculpture in our country that has led him into this diverse expression; but there is no doubt today that he is the only sculptor here to have produced a body of work over the years that is consistent in authenticity and understanding.’ (Gieve Patel, A.M. Davierwalla, Lalit Kala Contemporary Indian Art Series, New Delhi, 1971, unpaginated)
The current work, titled Surya Dev, made in steel and dated to 1967, was created as a study for a public sculpture of the same name, that was originally commissioned for the Ananta Co-operative Housing Society in Mumbai. The full- size version of Surya Dev (illustrated here in black and white), originally stood at a monumental twelve feet tall, but has, unfortunately, now been demolished. A comparison between the current maquette and the photograph of the final image reveals minor differences between the two sculptures. In the smaller study, the legs of the figure are formed of straight blocks that extend outwards from the bent knees. In the final work, the legs are no longer straight, but curved, with the lower legs turned inwards, giving the larger bronze a greater sense of upward momentum. Likewise, the neck of the final form seems somewhat elongated, with slight adaptions to the geometric blocks that form the detailing on the torso and hips of the figure. Pilloo Pochkhanawala – A Retrospective 1952-1984,
exhibition catalogue, Gallery Chemould, Mumbai, 1984, unpaginated, illustrated.
Pilloo Pochkhanawala is renowned for her experimental practices and exploration of metal alloys throughout her career. The current lot represents her mid-1970s experiments with mixtures of aluminium and varying proportions of nickel, silica and copper bronze. ‘Veering sharply away from the rigid architectonics of welded scrap metal into flowing sweeps of striated, scratch cast aluminium, Pochkhanawala’s enduring identity with the elemental forces of nature began to dominate her work.’ (Nikky Ty-Tomkins Seth, ‘Introduction’, Pilloo Pochkhanawala – A Retrospective 1952- 1984, exhibition catalogue, Gallery Chemould, Mumbai, 1984, unpaginated)
A Mumbai-based artist, Pochkhanawala helped bring sculpture to the fore within the painting-dominated environs of 20th century Indian art. While active in the art world from the 1950s to the 1980s, Pochkhanawala initially started with a commerce degree and a job in advertising. During a work trip to Europe in 1951, she visited several museums and discovered a connection to modern European sculpture, that she found lacking in her visual equation with paintings. ‘The paintings did not ruffle my inner composure. I was mortified by the sculptures because I was seized by the fear of the challenge of tackling something so difficult.’ (Pilloo Pochkhanawala in S.G. Vasudev’s introductory essay, Pilloo Pochkhanawala, New Delhi, unpaginated) Her sculptures ranged in scale from smaller intimate pieces to commissioned monumental public works of art. Discussing her smaller works around the time she created Grotto, she says, ‘My small metal sculptures have large-scale growth potential. After all, nature reveals its multiple patterns not only through its highest mountains, but even its smallest pebble. Every scar and scratch holds in it a tumultuous drama. It may have been a result of years of erosion or it may have b

literature_text:
Mohan Samant: mixed media works, exhibition catalogue, Pundole Art Gallery, Mumbai, 2008, unpaginated, illustrated.
‘In my painting I have swallowed the entire history of a thousands of years and synchronised it into a modern idiom. Nobody can tell me I am a copyist because I am just as modern as anyone else except that my influences do not come from a contemporary art world; they come from the entire panorama of art history.’ (Artist Statement, ‘Different strokes’, www.mid-day.com, 13th October, 2013)
Born in 1924, Mohan Samant grew up in Mumbai, India. Early in life, Samant realised that music and painting were his calling. ‘Stating, “I don’t want to go to college. I don’t want to work in an office. I want to learn to paint,” he enrolled at the Sir J.J. (Jamsetjee Jejeebhoy) School of Art in Bombay.’ (Marcella Sirhandi, ‘Mohan Samant (1924–2004)’, Mohan Samant: Paintings, Ahmedabad, 2013, p. 187) Samant was also an accomplished sarangi player. In 1952, he joined the Progressive Artists Group and after receiving the Rockefeller Fellowship, he moved to New York.
Whilst there, Samant immersed himself in the Abstract Expressionism that had taken over the artistic climate of the city in the 1950s and 1960s, but his strong fidelity toward his Indian modernist heritage instilled in him an affiliation to expressionist figuration and identifiable forms. However, with Abstract Expressionism emerging as an extremely influential movement, shifting the axis of power over to the United States from Europe for the first time, any excitement over Samant’s unique, non-native style was minimised. Samant shared the same fate as many other expatriate artists, fading from the chronicle of his homeland’s art history. As cultural theorist Ranjit Hoskote stated, Samant was ‘the missing link in the revolutionary narrative of contemporary art in India.’ (Ranjit Hoskote, Mohan Samant: mixed media works, exhibition catalogue, Pundole Art Gallery, Mumbai, 2008, unpaginated)

‘In a phase in Indian painting when there is an epidemic of figuration in the name of humanistic considerations, Krishen’s work has a consistent presence, free of any cult orientation. It is Krishen’s primary commitment to his medium that brings relevance to his subject matter in art and lifts the mundane to the realm of the poignant.’ (Jagdish Swaminathan, Krishen Khanna, exhibition catalogue, Centre for Contemporary Art, New Delhi, 1989, unpaginated)
The current painting, created in the early 1970s, depicts a seated female figure wrapped in magenta robes. With her left leg bent at the knee, and her right leg stretched out in front of her, she is seated on the ground supported by her right hand. Her shrouded face, lit with an ethereal light, is quietly contemplative, tinged with sadness. The work is titled Figure, and as the title suggests, it depicts an unknown person, for she is one of a multitude of anonymous labourers who begin to appear in Krishen Khanna’s canvases at this time. The characters from this broader theme include the exhausted men covered in cement dust as seen in the Nocturne series, the labourers crowded into the back of trucks from the Rear View series, and even the sleeping figures of the saints in Gethsamane. All these figures are unified by a sense of rootlessness, they are migrants in a hostile city which barely registers their presence, and only tolerates them for their ‘acts of excruciating labour.’
Gayatri Sinha explains, ‘...the genesis of these paintings lies in the troubled 1970s. In the massive influx of refugees in the aftermath of the Bangladesh war and India’s failing socialist political model... Khanna’s studies of refugees of the 1940s and 50s now mutate in the migrant figures trapped in the hustling onrush of the growing metro. Their drapes of mainly unstitched cloth that protect their faces against the dust and fumes of the street identify them as rural migrants – probably fleeing the village as a c

‘From forlorn men to desolate landscapes is but a step and Ram Kumar takes this step in 1960 which marks the beginning of a new phase in his work. In one painting of this year of transition the landscape indeed only reiterates and reinforces the sense of anguish which the faces convey. It would have done as well by itself. Ram Kumar sees the point and leaves hereafter the human face alone.’ (Shamlal, Ram Kumar, New Delhi, 1968, p. iv)
Upon his return from France to India in the early 1950s, Ram Kumar focused on figurative works, where he chose to portray the monotony and decay of urban existence. The works from this period reflect his personal disillusionment with society and Indian politics, but form part of a larger artistic commentary on the despair and desolation experienced in India after Independence. Ram’s figures, despite sometimes appearing huddled in family groups, rarely display any sign of tenderness or interaction with each other. As the decade progresses, his figures start to diminish in scale, and by late 1958, they have become abstracted, sometimes condensed down to geometric shapes that merely reflect the spiritless structures of the city that surrounds them.
The current work, dated to 1959, belongs to this post- figurative phase, where any semblance of humanity has been replaced by random forms that will slowly crystallise into abstract landscapes over the coming years. Executed primarily in shades of grey and white, the palette also lacks the areas of vermilion used in other works from this period, perhaps a precursor to the predominantly ‘grey’ period that was to follow, a reflection of the growing desolation he observed in his surroundings. This particular composition is possibly the perfect link between the figurative works of the earlier years and semi-representational landscapes such as Greek Landscape (1961) that came right after.
