‘When we look at these creatures we must remember that the animal is not the subject of Husain’s painting, it is the demonic principle that he depicts which is neither good nor bad. The nudes and the horses and elephants have become symbols of power and pursuit, or of mysterious encounters. Of course, not all the paintings carry this message. But if the painting is structured rightly and if it holds our attention and if we can admire the method by which the parts have been put together, we need not worry about the implications. Paint does not lie. It is capable of revealing the prowess of the painter. If it does this, the painting holds, and what art we see will depend on the precise insights we have about art and on our ability to look squarely at things.’ (Richard Bartholomew, Husain, New York, 1971, p. 20)
Much has been written on M.F. Husain’s horses, his female figures, and the relationship between these two iconic subjects for the artist. The horse and nude, both, have fascinated Husain since the 1950s. The pictorial vocabulary of the horse has evolved through an amalgamation of several varied visual memories; from riding in his grandfather’s horse-drawn tonga as a child, to absorbing images of the stylised Duldul horse during annual Muharram processions in Indore, to the horse in Chinese painting and sculpture from his first visit to the country in 1952. The earliest nudes were often inspired by classical Indian sculpture, which he consistently studied through his career, be it at the exhibition in New Delhi he visited with F.N. Souza in 1948, or his visit to Khajuraho in the 1950s, or to the Government Museum in Chennai to admire the Chola bronzes in the 1970s.
Husain painted countless works of both these subjects independently, but it is the juxtaposition of these two themes, specifically, that Husain was especially interested in, and experimented with consistently through his career, each time achieving something different and

title: Untitled
literature_text:
Mriga, Animal in Indian Art, exhibition catalogue, The Festival of India, New Delhi, 1988, pp. 69 & 107 illustrated.
For a comparable South Indian bronze depicting Garuda from a similar period, see Vidya Dehejia, Chola Sacred Bronzes of Southern India, London, 2007, p. 131.
This bronze Garuda is likely to have once been part of a larger bronze grouping of Lord Krishna, with his consorts Rukmini and Satyabhama. The majestic grouping relates to the final phase of Krishna’s life on Earth, when he ruled as king of Dvarka in western India. Here, Garuda, Vishnu’s vahana is portrayed in human form, but with a beaked nose, fangs and wings. Garuda stands on a double lotus pedestal; his hands clasping a lotus, are held in anjali mudra in front of his chest in adoration of his Lord. He is clad in a short dhoti, with an elaborate waistband or katisutra composed of strips of ribbon, which are held in place by a lion-headed clasp. He is adorned with multiple necklaces, bracelets and armbands; his head surmounted by a conical multi-tiered crown, is backed by a shirashchakra of lotus pattern form, encircled by a single band. His tightly curling locks of hair fall in a neat row at the nape of his neck and are bordered by a band of one of his necklaces. A striking feature of this particular depiction of Garuda, is the diamond-shaped pattern which covers his chest, shoulders and upper arms; each diamond incised with feather-shaped detailing, further emphasising his identity as Vishnu’s divine eagle mount.
Garuda’s perennial enmity with the nagas is sometimes reflected in his iconography, as he is often seen holding a snake in his beak or in one of his talons. Here, this feature is absent, but the nagas retain their presence by forming part of his jewellery. One is wrapped around his crown, while others are present as earrings and necklaces. Such is his dominion over these feared beings, that one even takes the place of his sacred thread.

title: Untitled
literature_text:
Nancy Adajania, ‘Sculpture at the Edge’, Art India, volume 5, issue 3, Mumbai, quarter 3, 2000, p. 27, illustrated.
The Sculpted Image A Panorama of Contemporary Indian Sculpture Bombay Art Festival ’87, exhibition catalogue, Nehru Centre, Mumbai, 1987, unpaginated, illustrated.
Pilloo Pochkhanawala–A Retrospective 1952-1984, exhibition catalogue, Gallery Chemould, Mumbai, 1984, unpaginated, illustrated.
Proceeds from the sale of the following two Pilloo Pochkhanawala sculptures will go to the Kadri Foundation to benefit the education of girls and support medical needs for the underprivileged.
‘Flow and rhythm and energy is what my sculpture is all about.’ (Pilloo Pochkhanawala in an interview with Lakshmi Lal, ‘The Dare and the Discipline’, The Times of India, Mumbai, 19 February, 1984, p. A2)
A Mumbai-based artist, Pilloo Pochkhanawala was one of the first female Indian sculptors of her kind. Pochkhanawala helped bring sculpture across the painting-dominated frontiers of 20th century Indian art. While active in the art world from the 1950s to the 1980s, Pochkhanawala initially started with a commerce degree and a job in advertising. During a work trip to Europe in 1951, she visited several museums and felt a connection to modern European sculpture, that she found lacking in her visual equation with paintings. ‘The paintings did not ruffle my inner composure. I was mortified by the sculptures because I was seized by the fear of the challenge of tackling something so difficult.’ (Pilloo Pochkhanawala in S.V. Vasudev’s introductory essay, Pilloo Pochkhanawala, New Delhi, unpaginated)
So began Pochkhanawala’s career-long experimentation with tactile form, anchored by her interest in industrial materials. N.G. Pansare mentored her alongside her contemporary, Adi Davierwalla. Pansare provided her with the technical foundation to explore wood, scrap iron, steel, lead and cement; she culminated her sculptural evolution with aluminum alloy, her favoured material of choice.
exhibited_text:
The Sculpted Image A Panorama of Contemporary Indian Sculpture Bombay Art Festival ’87, Nehru Centre, Mumbai, 15 - 30 November, 1987.
Pilloo Pochkhanawala–A Retrospective 1952-1984, Gallery Chemould, Mumbai, 1984.

title: Untitled
literature_text:
Pilloo Pochkhanawala–A Retrospective 1952-1984, exhibition catalogue, Gallery Chemould, Mumbai, 1984, unpaginated, illustrated.
Pilloo Pochkhanawala grew up on the shores of Zanzibar looking out into the vast Indian Ocean. ‘My early childhood was spent in Zanzibar. We lived on the seashore. I love the sea and yet was never comfortable with it...’ (As quoted by the artist, S.V. Vasudev, Pilloo Pochkhanawala, New Delhi, dust jacket) The stretches of the Swahili coast, particularly the warm skies on the endless horizon of the ocean, likely served as inspiration for the artist. The sky as subject is also prevalent in Cloud Burst (lot 15), a part of her Monsoon series.
Pochkhanawala is renowned for her experimental practices and exploration of metal alloys throughout her career. The current lot represents her mid-1970s experiments with mixtures of aluminum and varying proportions of nickel, silica and copper bronze. ‘Veering sharply away from the rigid architectonics of welded scrap metal into flowing sweeps of striated, scratch cast aluminum, Pochkhanawala’s enduring identity with the elemental forces of nature began to dominate her work.’ (Nikky Ty-Tomkins Seth, ‘Introduction’, Pilloo Pochkhanawala – A Retrospective 1952- 1984, exhibition catalogue, Gallery Chemould, Mumbai, 1984, unpaginated)
Lyrical curved sweeps of aluminum make up the landscape, half-crescents adjacent to the pillar-like forms on the left. Typical of her later works, this work is geometric in appearance and bold in gesture, and similar forms reappear in several sculptures. These iconic, arced shapes also appear in her drawings of the early 1970s. These drawings were never the end goal, but served as an experimental space to visualise the third dimension. (For an example of a drawing illustrating similar forms, see Pundole’s, The Works on Paper Sale including Books and the Nandalal Bose Archive, Mumbai, 11 April, 2019, lot 72.) Collectively referred to as the Metal-Scapes, the current Skyscape work
exhibited_text: Pilloo Pochkhanawala–A Retrospective 1952-1984, Gallery Chemould, Mumbai, 1984.

title: Marble
provenance_text:
Formerly in the Collection of Mr. S.H. Daya.
Trained in Baroda and London, the national award-winning sculptor, Ramesh Pateria, brought a new visual language to marble, a material traditionally associated with classical sculptures in Europe and elegant architectural elements in the Indian context. Pateria favoured marble from the quarries of Makrana in Rajasthan, a site that supplied to monuments like the Taj Mahal in Agra and the Victoria Memorial in Kolkata. However, his sculptures were not monumental— they were abstract meditations on form — architectural, organic, or human.
The current sculpture in marble combines the rough texture of unpolished marble with a highly polished figure that emerges from the stone. The nude female figure has angular hips and sensually rounded breasts with an elongated hour-glass shaped torso; modern interpretations that hearken back to favoured attributes from Indian classical sculpture. The three holes at the base of her throat suggest the idea of a piece of jewellery, but like her features, have been pared down from their visually elaborate predecessors.

title: Untitled
literature_text:
Geeta Doctor, ‘Crafting a New Art’, Art India, volume XVI, issue III, Mumbai, December 2011, p. 35, illustrated.
Frontal Narrative Sculptures 2011 S. Nandagopal, exhibition catalogue, Art World, Chennai, 2011, unpaginated, illustrated.
The Museum of the Madras Movement, exhibition catalogue, Chennai, 2016, cover and unpaginated, illustrated.
‘With so much of Indian cultural and aesthetic tradition behind me, I find it both a privilege and challenge to draw from the vital Indian inspiration and fuse the apparent contradiction into an acceptable form.’ (Shankar Nandagopal in conversation with Ian Findlay-Brown, ‘The Flight of Form’, World Sculpture News, volume 12, number 1, Hong Kong, Winter 2006, p. 27)
Like many Indian sculptors, Nandagopal first began his career as a painter. However, while attending a mandatory course in craftwork, the young artist found himself spending most of his time in the craft studio, and quickly discovered his métier in sculpture. Shortly after completing his studies, Nandagopal moved to the Cholamandal Artists’ Village in Adyar, Chennai, which was founded by his father, K.C.S. Paniker, one of the most important artists of the Madras Art Movement and the 20th century.
The artists working in Chennai (then Madras) in the 1950s and 1960s sought to establish a distinct regional modernist identity. In a similar vein, Nandagopal sought to integrate his unique interpretation of the formal discoveries in modern art, with traditional South Indian sculptural practices in a way that was relevant to contemporary India. Using materials such as copper sheets, bronze, steel, and enamel, Nandagopal’s oeuvre primarily consists of fully frontal, primarily two-dimensional sculptures on which he imposes intricate surface detail on the external surface.
His ‘pictorial sculptures’ underwent a radical transformation through the incorporation of contemporary materials, a change that extended to the content of his sculptures. Despite the artist’s reluctance to ex

title: Untitled
literature_text:
Ranjit Hoskote, ‘Akbar Padamsee Between the Hieratic and the Human’, Art Heritage 11, New Delhi, 1992, p. 137, illustrated.
For Akbar Padamsee, faces have consistently served as a catalyst for exploration. In conversation about his work, he said, ‘my theme has always been people and portraits. The human figure and the face are all themes I am obsessed with ... Expression is more powerful when it is about a solitary figure or just a face ... Faces portray the character of a person. It is that which basically differentiates one from the other. And in God’s unique creations, no two faces are alike. In my creative endeavour, I love to go for that unusual one which stands apart in a crowd of faces. However, I paint all these faces from my mind and they seldom resemble a known character.’ (Arup K. Datta, Padamsee, exhibition catalogue, Sanskriti Art Gallery, Kolkata, 1992, unpaginated)
Padamsee’s pursuit of painting realistic heads, with multi- dimensional, almost sculptural qualities, ultimately translated into his creating tangible, three-dimensional forms. Between 1984 and 1985, the artist sculpted two hundred heads in plaster, out of which he selected eight to be cast in bronze, including the present example. ‘Modelled like death masks, the taut, wizened faces rendered in craggy, fractured planes deeply gauges and pitted.’ (Ella Dutta, ‘Pictorial Chronology’, Art Heritage 8, New Delhi, 1988-89, p. 55)

title: Untitled
literature_text:
Richard Bartholomew and Shiv S. Kapur, Husain, New York, 1972, p. 191, illustrated.
In reference to Husain’s paintings on space exploration, Ebrahim Alkazi wrote, ‘Man’s Exploration of outer space is linked with other miraculous achievements, like the building of the pyramids, the development of such sciences as mathematics, astronomy, navigation, and so on. Each in its own way was a search for truth and for the basic principles underlying the cosmological order.’ (Ebrahim Alkazi, ‘The Metaphysical Dimension’, M.F. Husain: The Modern Artist and Tradition, Art Heritage, New Delhi, p.28)
Born out of a tactile interplay with life, M.F Husain’s artworks exist as interpretive insights into the current events of their time. Consistently engaged with the reality around him, the artist’s oeuvre encompassed the ordinary and the monumental. Larger than life figures who shaped history feature prominently: Husain painted a series of works on Mother Teresa after she had been awarded the Nobel Peace Prize, on Mahatma Gandhi after he was assassinated, and on Indira Gandhi during the controversial period of the Emergency in the 1970s. He also used his canvas to respond to major events that he had either witnessed, such as the Bangladesh War in 1971, or experienced vicariously, such as his series on the Moon Landing in 1969.
While an estimated six hundred and fifty million people watched, Apollo 11’s Lunar Module Eagle (LM-5) landed on the moon. The present lot, Moon Landing, is the only known sculpture created amongst a suite of paintings by Husain to mark this extraordinary technological achievement and investment in the pursuit of knowledge. Moon Landing, fashioned out of found metal objects, loosely resembles the LM-5. Its long pipe-like legs angle outward from the perforated, cylindrical body of the spacecraft. While the doors open to an empty space, an image of the sculpture published in the iconic Harry Abrams book on the artist shows a doll placed inside with one leg steppin
exhibited_text: M.F. Husain, Gallery Chanakya, New Delhi, July-August, 1969.

title: Terracotta
literature_text:
Gieve Patel Sculptures and Drawing, exhibition catalogue, The Guild Art Gallery, Mumbai, 2007, unpaginated, illustrated.
Sculpsit: Between Thought and Action, exhibition catalogue, The Guild Art Gallery, Mumbai, 2022, pp. 19, 105 and 112, illustrated.
Seventeen years ago, the painter, playwright and poet, Gieve Patel, who recently passed away, added sculptor to the list of his artistic métier with Eklavya and Daphne, a series of pieces in bronze, fibreglass, and terracotta. The artist explored myths of transformation and violence as his own practice changed form. What remained constant was Patel’s engagement with nature, landscape and storytelling. His other career — that of a physician —ensured that these works were strongly rooted in the human condition, depicting passion and pain, and examining physiognomy.
In the Daphne series, Patel explored the transformation of Daphne into a laurel tree to escape Apollo’s advances, transferring themes of horror, fear, and fertility into sculptures. The horned face is bleached white, like a forgotten, deformed skeleton, with a sensuous and articulated mouth. Horns creep too close to where the eyes would have been, but there are neither hollows nor a socket, just undulations, leading to a lumpy frontal bone. Pressing into the terracotta, Patel conveys lust, pain, perhaps even remorse.
Ranjit Hoskote explains in an exhibition catalogue essay, ‘Patel’s is a haptic consciousness, the grip and grasp not merely holding but also shaping the world’s material into an image. He corroborates this view, taking delight in the gestural nature of the new work: “I’ve used a lump of clay as an ear, a gash as a mouth. The clay has the capacity to speak, I just have to nudge it.”’ (Ranjit Hoskote, ‘To Break and To Branch: Gieve Patel’s ‘Eklavya/ Daphne’’, Eklavya Daphne, exhibition catalogue, The Guild, Mumbai, 2007, unpaginated)
exhibited_text:
Sculpsit: Between Thought and Action, Sunaparanta Goa Centre for the Arts, Goa, 4 October - 22 December, 2019.
Sculpsit: Between Thought and Action, The Guild Art Gallery, Mumbai, 12 April - 25 May, 2019.

title: Acrylic on canvas
literature_text:
Udayan Vajpeyi and Vivek, Jangarh Kalam, Bhopal, 2005, p. 132, illustrated.
Jangarh Singh Shyam is the flagbearer of Pardhan-Gond art. Having grown up in Patangarh, Madhya Pradesh in abject poverty, Shyam quit school and began working as a farmer at a very young age. In 1981, while decorating the walls of huts in Patangarh with traditional motifs, Shyam was approached by young research students from Bharat Bhavan, a multi-platform art museum in Bhopal. Jagdish Swaminathan, the founder of the Museum, had sent these scouts to discover innovative young talent from rural areas. This encounter led Shyam to meet and develop a close relationship with Swaminathan, who showcased his artworks at Bharat Bhavan’s inaugural exhibition in February 1982. Through his artistry, Jangarh Singh Shyam has formed a type of pictorial narrative that has developed into a movement and helped pave the way for a new school of Indian art, now referred to as ‘Jangarh Kalam’.
While cultivating his own personal idiom, Shyam created a new visual language rooted in Pardhan-Gond traditions which exhibited a contemporary aesthetic. Borrowing from his rich cultural inheritance, including mystical Gond folktales, vignettes of the abundant flora, fauna, and avifauna from the forests of Madhya Pradesh have emerged in Shyam’s artworks, as seen in lots 4, 5 and 6.
In a quasi-pointillist depiction of two crocodile companions, Shyam superimposes dotted, scallop-like patterns onto flat colour planes, the placement of which echoes the rhythmic vibrations of Pardhan music. Deliberately left blank, the background contrasts with the vibrantly rendered subjects and is balanced out by the negative space in the white of the crocodiles’ eyes. Crocodiles and other reptiles including snakes and lizards appear frequently in Shyam’s works. ‘The ‘magar’ or crocodile is often an important character in Indian stories and legends. There is no doubt that the animal was also pertinent to the Gond tribe’s rich folklore and tradi

title: Untitled
provenance_text: The current lot was acquired by Fritz Schleicher in 1903, when he purchased the Ravi Varma Fine Arts Lithographic Press and its contents from the artist Raja Ravi Varma.
literature_text:
Rupika Chawla, Raja Ravi Varma Painter of Colonial India, Ahmedabad, 2010, p. 231 and p. 259, illustrated.
Raja Ravi Varma and the Fine Arts Lithographic Press
Raja Ravi Varma was born Ravi Varma Koil Thampuran in Kilimanoor village, in the erstwhile state of Travancore in South India. The family of Kilimanoor, to which he belonged, had very close ties with the Travancore royal family, thereby allowing him to have a close relationship with them. Following traditional norms, Ravi Varma was educated in the Kilimanoor Palace. However, in addition to the regular curriculum, his uncle, sensing a special talent, added a special class of drawing for the young boy, ‘...even at the tender age of five or six, he would cram the walls of his home with pictures of animals and scenes from daily life done with chalk and charcoal, despite the scoldings he regularly got for ‘spoiling’ the walls.’ (Parsram Mangharam, Raja Ravi Varma The Painter Prince 1848 - 1906, Bangalore, 2003, p. 17)
Ravi Varma grew up in an environment rich with the arts. The Royal family of Travancore had always been active patrons of the arts, including music, painting and sculpture, and his proximity to the family ensured that he was exposed to these forms from a young age. His skill as a draughtsman was spotted at the age of thirteen by the Maharaja, who had been presented with drawings by Ravi Varma, and was thoroughly impressed. He instructed the boy to live in the palace and learn from the other artists working in the court at the time. The Indian court artists had recently been introduced to two novel concepts: canvas and oil paint, both alien to the artists of South India, but critical elements to the European academic style that was currently in favour. As they were still learning how to make optimal use of these magical mediums, there was a fair amount of caution and jealousy that existed amongst the artists. None were willing to be generous with their time or knowledge, and the young Ravi Varma lacke

title: Untitled
provenance_text: The current lot was acquired by Fritz Schleicher in 1903, when he purchased the Ravi Varma Fine Arts Lithographic Press and its contents from the artist Raja Ravi Varma.
literature_text:
Rupika Chawla, Raja Ravi Varma Painter of Colonial India, Ahmedabad, 2010, p. 156, illustrated.
The current lot, titled Rama, Sita and Laksmana Crossing the Sarayu or Ram Vanavas (Rama in Exile) is one of a series of religious paintings that Raja Ravi Varma created depicting scenes from the Ramayana . The composition was produced as an oleograph at the Ravi Varma Fine Arts Lithographic Press in 1898, and the art historian Rupika Chawla has suggested that this canvas is likely to have been painted by the artist as the exemplar for the print. It depicts Rama, Sita and Laksmana at the final moment of their departure from Ayodhya, which signified the beginning of their fourteen years of exile from the court.
Rama and Laksmana have disposed of their royal regalia and now wear plain dhotis, their hair drawn up in matted locks, in the style of sadhus. Sita, clearly apprehensive of what is to come and the journey ahead, leans her head on Rama’s shoulder for consolation and support. The act of physically crossing the river in a boat also represents a symbolic crossing, or moment of transition, for the trio, from courtly life to one of exile and banishment in the forest; it is the point of no return. Reaching the far banks of the river signifies the geographical shift from courtly grandeur to a nomadic life, as well as the spiritual evolution from the life of a householder to one of an ascetic. The painting, thus, highlights the important emotional journey that they are about to undertake.
Despite the use of academic realism, some critics have noted that in many of Ravi Varma’s larger mythological or Puranic paintings, there is a tendency, at first glance, to read his compositions as being overtly staged, the gestures of his main figures too theatrical, and often, the action itself, artificially frozen in time. His complicated crowd scenes ‘look like stills from theatre productions, and from very provincial productions at that, where actors are overacting in awkward dresses b

title: Untitled
provenance_text:
The current lot was acquired by Fritz Schleicher in 1903, when he purchased the Ravi Varma Fine Arts Lithographic Press and its contents from the artist Raja Ravi Varma.
Ravi Varma and his brother both spent a considerable amount of time in Mumbai in the periods preceding and following the opening of the Ravi Varma Fine Arts Lithographic Press, both for of the cultural and visual stimulus the city provided, as well as for its vibrant energy and ‘brilliant assembly’ of local residents that held serious potential for painting commissions. Whilst in Mumbai, the brothers lived and worked in a building in Girgaum. Like so many other elements in the city, Ravi Varma drew particular inspiration from a temple in the area dedicated to Dattatreya, ‘one of the lesser-known gods but in whom faith has been prevalent in Maharashtra for centuries... There are not too many temples dedicated to Dattatreya, making it quite likely that this is the same one Ravi Varma would have seen several times as he moved about in the area. He adapted the three-dimensional statue of the deity in a flat painted image, which saw further conversion into an oleograph at the printing press.’ (Rupika Chawla, Raja Ravi Varma Painter of Colonial India, Ahmedabad, 2010, p. 97)
Dattatreya, or the Universal Guru, is Trimurti or the three- headed incarnation of Brahma, Vishnu and Shiva. Certain scriptures also refer to this particular manifestation as Parabrahma or the Supreme Being. In the current painting, Dattatreya is shown holding a combination of all their attributes, and is surrounded by the omnipresent cow and his four dogs. The wish-fulfilling cow, or Kamadhenu, represents Mother Earth and Dharma, whilst each of the dogs stand for one of the Vedas, the external storehouses of Spiritual Wisdom. In his multiple hands, he holds the trident, the Sudarshan chakra, the conch, a lotus flower, the bhasma or ashes and the begging bowl, each of which serve as symbols of the lives each of the three Supreme Being

title: Untitled
medium_raw: Ink, watercolour and gouache on paper
literature_text:
K.K. Hebbar, Voyage in Images, Mumbai, 1991, unpaginated, pl. 5, illustrated.
Bombay Art Society 55th Annual Exhibition, Mumbai, 1946, p. 11, illustrated.
Kattingeri Krishna Hebbar’s ‘early works show the active life spirit hidden within the rustic village folk and the ordinary people of his surroundings.’ (Hebbar An Artist’s Quest, exhibition catalogue, National Gallery of Modern Art, Bengaluru, 2011, p. 31) At this point in time, Hebbar desired to part with the academic style he was lauded for in the early 1940s, and, inspired by Ananda Coomaraswamy’s writings on Indian art, he decided to experiment with gouache and tempera. As the artist felt that he had already mastered the use of oil paint, he decided to change mediums, and chose to work in smaller formats, seeking inspiration from Indian manuscript, miniature, and mural painting traditions. At the same time, he shifted his focus onto everyday, rural subjects, rather than deified and elite ones. The current group of three early, small format paintings (lots 18-20) depict these very scenes of a charmed rural life; some capturing larger community celebrations around festivals, while others are endearing depictions of village children.
Festival Dance (Kulu Valley) first appeared at the Bombay Art Society’s 55th Annual Exhibition in 1946, where the artist was awarded a bronze medal in the Watercolour (Indian Style) category. However, at the time of the work’s creation, such prizes were of secondary concern for the artist. In his essay for the retrospective exhibition titled Voyage in Images, held at the Jehangir Art Gallery in 1991, the artist noted that this work stood out within his oeuvre from this early period in his career. To Hebbar’s observant eye and lyrical hand, music, dance, and rural life became core themes for his paintings. In Festival Dance (Kulu Valley), musicians are the focal point around which a communal gathering and performance emerge. Unlike his later works, in which musicians, dancers, and in
exhibited_text: Bombay Art Society 55th Annual Exhibition, Mumbai, 1946.

title: Untitled
literature_text:
Jamshed Bhabha, Ed., Homi Bhabha as Artist A Selection of his Paintings, Drawings and Sketches, Mumbai, 1968, unpaginated, pl.5, illustrated.
Born in Mumbai in 1909, Homi Bhabha had ‘the good fortune of being brought up from a very early age in what may be described as an atmosphere of learning.’ (Jamshed Bhabha, ‘Introduction’, Homi Bhabha as Artist, Mumbai, 1968, p. 1) His family’s library, curated by his educationist grandfather Hormusji, was stocked with books on history, literature, and poetry, along with a variety of illustrated books on the European old masters. From a young age, Bhabha received art lessons in the traditional academic style, and won several prizes for artists under eighteen in the annual exhibitions of the Bombay Art Society. Thanks to his aunt, Miss Cooma Panday, Bhabha gained access to an impressive collection of gramophone records, and by the age of sixteen, Bhabha also developed a profound love for classical music.
In 1927, Bhabha went to Cambridge University to study Engineering, where the brilliant physicists and mathematicians he encountered at the Cavendish library made a lasting impression on him, which led to him studying Mathematics after attaining a first-class degree in Engineering. After graduating, Bhabha pursued a career in Physics, and soon distinguished himself as the father of the Indian Nuclear Program. Bhabha was the founding director and professor for Physics at the Tata Institute of Fundamental Research, as well as the founding director of the Atomic Energy Establishment, and was the first chairman of the Indian Atomic Energy Commission, as well as secretary of the Department of Atomic Energy.
In the Introduction to the book Homi Bhabha as Artist, his brother, Dr. Jamshed Bhabha, wrote, ‘Homi Bhabha was a born artist even though his primary pre-occupation with science did not leave him sufficient time perhaps to develop fully his potential capacity.’ (ibid., p. 6.) ‘For Homi Bhabha, the arts were not just a form of recr

title: Untitled
literature_text:
Debdutta Gupta, Postcards by Nandalal Bose, exhibition catalogue, Akar Prakar, Kolkata, 2011, unpaginated, illustrated.
The following two lots are original illustrations to a children’s play, Tak Duma Dum, written by Jnanadanandini Tagore, who was married to Satyendranath Tagore, Rabindranath Tagore’s eldest brother. The author is best known as a social reformer, who influenced the earliest phase of women’s empowerment in 19th Century Bengal, and she also participated in the rich literary traditions of the Tagore family, writing articles for the Bengali journal Bharati. In 1885, Jnanadanandini Devi established Balak, the first children’s literary magazine in Bengali, to which Rabindranath contributed a number of poems, plays, and short stories. Jnanadanandini also wrote for the publication; notably, two plays, Tak Duma Dum and Sat Bhai Champa, both of which were highly regarded in literary circles at the time.
Tak Duma Dum revolves around a jackal who gets thorns stuck in his whiskers when trying to steal aubergines from the village barber’s vegetable patch. The jackal approaches the barber to ask for his assistance in getting the thorns out, but as the barber uses his tweezers, he hurts the jackal. As compensation for his discomfort, the barber gives the jackal the tweezers. Later, the jackal sees a potter in his garden trying to dig up the soil with his bare hands. The jackal, in an attempt to help, offers him his newly acquired tweezers, which promptly break, and the potter offers him a pot as compensation for his loss. Moving on, the jackal meets a gardener trying to water his plants with his hands, so yet again, to be helpful, he offers him the use of his pot, but alas, the gardener breaks the pot. In exchange for the broken pot, he gives the jackal a Bengali groom’s wedding hat called a Topor. title: Untitled
literature_text:
Francesca Souza, Ed., F.N. Souza Religion & Erotica, Early Religious and Erotic Drawings from the Estate of F.N. Souza, London, 2005, p. 50, illustrated.
A century after Titian’s masterpiece, Portrait of Pope Paul III, Spanish painter Velázquez studied the painting and requested permission to paint Pope X while in Rome. Pope Innocent X, regarded as one of the most controversial popes in history, was known to be hot-tempered, untrusting, ruthless, merciless, and, therefore, seemed conceited in taking the name ‘Innocent’.
A critic of the Catholic Church, and a devout atheist, Francis Bacon painted Study after Velasquez’s Portrait of Pope Innocent X in 1953. ‘This terrifying image that depicts the tortured expression of a blood-splattered Pope imprisoned in... a bare metal throne is known as the Screaming Pope, the open mouth is central to it. Bacon is known to have commented, “I have always been very moved by the movements of the mouth, the shape of the mouth and the teeth.”’ (Aziz Kurtha, Francis Newton Souza: Bridging Western and Indian Modern Art, Ahmedabad, 2006, p. 44)
Bacon and Souza had often gone out drinking together at the Colony Room after it opened in Soho, London in 1949. On seeing them, Muriel Belcher, the owner of the private club, would ‘sharply callout “Francis!” causing both of them to respond.’ (Victor Rangel-Riberio, Souza: The Man, the Artist, his Loves and his Times, Goa, 2019, p. 100)
Sketched in 1955, the present lot certainly refers to Bacon’s painting of the Pope painted in 1953. The teeth and mouth, though not screaming like Bacon’s, have definite prominence, as is seen in several of Souza’s works. However, the composition and garments of Souza’s pope bears closer resemblance to Velázquez’s figure in appearance and essence. Velázquez’s suspicion of Pope Innocent X would have been in concurrence with Souza’s own distrust of figures of religious authority and his complex relationship with the Catholic Church.

literature_text:
Mala Marwah, ‘Akbar Padamsee - A Conversation’, Lalit Kala Contemporary 23, New Delhi, April 1977, p. 35, illustrated.
Bhanumati Padamsee and Annapurna Garimella, (Eds.), Akbar Padamsee - Work in Language, Mumbai, 2010, p. 256 and p. 353, illustrated.
India: Myth & Reality Aspects of Modern Indian Art, exhibition catalogue, Museum of Modern Art, Oxford, 1982, p. 1 and p. 17, illustrated.
Ella Datta describes Akbar Padamsee’s vast, ruminative landscapes of the 1970s: ‘A sea-change in the Metascape paintings. Large, expansive fields of colour recalling mountains, fields, sky and water with the sun and the moon and their reflections. Strongly meditative in character and in brilliant colours, they echo the order and rationality that the mind wishes to impose on the dark, swirling depths hinted beyond the edge. Sublimity in conception and execution.’ (Ella Datta, ‘Pictorial Chronology’, Art Heritage 8, New Delhi, 1988-1989, p. 51)
Studying the numerous changes in his painting style from the 1950s onwards, and his periodic shifts between figurative and landscape painting, Datta makes a pertinent observation. By her analysis, there are always indicative signs that signal an inflection point in Padamsee’s creative process that consequently result in new painterly experiments. His two films from 1969, SYZYGY and Events in a Cloud Chamber, were closely related to the utterly cerebral Metascapes that would emerge a few years later. ‘In SYZYGY... Padamsee has created a set of moving forms which he has correlated with numbers. He has first created mathematical grids which he has programmed into visual forms that have been set in motion. This cerebral exercise is the base of his style and these programmed drawings are the most abstract of his works. In the Cloud Chamber film he has achieved, through purely technical means within the workshop, evocations of natural space and its changing aspects.’ (Ella Datta, ‘Akbar Padamsee: The Spirit of Order’, ibid., p. 37)

Exhibition of Paintings of Sadanand K. Bakre, Gallery Chemould, Mumbai, 19-26 March, 1965.
‘After a number of years in which the imagery was centered primarily on the human form, in recent years – since about 1965 – Bakre has introduced architectural motifs into his painting. Where the earlier figure-inspired work was soft and the drawing made in terms of the loaded brush, these later works are necessarily harder, the drawn line that much the more apparent and clearcut – the difference, in fact, between flesh and stone. After the years of expressive painterly (malerisch) brushwork the line now contains a more directly controlled consciousness of architectural forms. He proceeds across the surface in a relational series of box-like and triangular shapes which are slowly formed into houses, churches and streets. It is now that Bakre reveals himself as a more urban painter in work which does not hold the pastoral lyrical elements present in the art of Rama Rao, Viren Sahai and Balraj Khanna. Nevertheless, there remains that mastery of his craft which reveals line and paint combining to an expressive control of calculated power.’ (Bruce Jamies, ‘A Passage from India – Seven Painters in London’, Lalit Kala Contemporary 9, New Delhi, September 1968, p. 27)

‘Zarina is a thinking person’s artist. Her practice appears calm, quiet, collected and free of the desire for spectacular effects and monumentality, something that really cannot be said about a lot of globally distributed South Asian art. She seems intent on inviting emotional responses that lead to reflection and self-reflection, forms of sentiment and feeling that catalyse the process of becoming aware of ourselves and our place in the world. It is a sensibility that is shaped by (and is scrupulously attentive to) the social conflicts of our times. She asks us to be respectful of the finitude of our lives—an awareness of finality that she never allows to be sentimentalised. Her work displays a distinctive habit of reflection on what it means to be alive and to be human—not despite, but precisely in the midst of, the antagonisms and violence that are so omnipresent in our world.’ (Aamir R. Mufti, ‘Zarina’s Language Question’, Marg A Magazine of the Arts, vol. 48, no. 2, Mumbai, September - December 2016, p. 33)

‘One cannot walk into a roomful of Souzas without at once being forced to participate in certain passions and fears which make these violent distortions of the visual world explicable and sympathetic. Frequently these passions are not only violent but destructive, as though each painting liberated the artist from a private night-mare. His art is full of strange perversities and contradictions, too... all his most successful work seems to contain something of an emotional clash – vulgarity and tenderness, or agony and wit, pathos and satire, aggression and composure. They have some of the sheer inventiveness of Picasso ... and the same unresolved tumult.’ (Edwin Mullins, The Human and Divine Predicament, exhibition catalogue, Grosvenor Gallery, London, 1964, unpaginated)
By the early 1960s, Souza had achieved both fame and critical acclaim in London. In 1964, a leading critic, Mervyn Levy, described him as ‘one of the most vigorously stimulating and committed painters of our time.’ (F. N. Souza, ‘The Human and the Divine’, Studio International Art, London, April 1964, p. 134) Souza held important shows at both Gallery One and Grosvenor Gallery, and between 1962 and 1963, he created some of his most iconic works. These include The Butcher, The Remark, Beasts of Prey, The Deposition and The Red Curse. The current work, Hunger, belongs to this same group, and shares with these works, elements that generate in its audience, a sense of revulsion tinged with an intangible pathos.

‘You’ve got to think three-dimensionally in undefined space. Actually, given the choice, I would rather sculpt than paint.’ (The artist in conversation with Yashodhara Dalmia, Tyeb Mehta Ideas, Images, Exchanges, New Delhi, 2005, p. 353)
In the 1980s, Tyeb Mehta used bronze for the first time to express his artistic concerns. The bull and the figure, both re-appeared in radically different yet equally impactful forms, bearing a gravitas that captured their creator’s dilemmas. The bronze faces, as seen in this unique piece from 1985, retain the powerful expressions captured in their two-dimensional painted counterparts. The human form has never played a narrative role in Tyeb’s works, whether painting or sculpture. It is merely the vehicle, through which both the artist and viewer, can contemplate man’s existence and examine the violence and suffering that mankind relentlessly endures.
‘Just like the artist has internalised these experiences, his protagonists too encrypt these acts of provocation as well as the endurance necessary to survive them. The manic razor of the riot, the visitation of terror in the crowded streets, the spilling of blood glimpsed through the half-open window: in India, every festivity is edged with panic, every luminescence is shadowed by memories of torture, and no identity is so stable that it can secure itself against its own interrogations.’ (Ranjit Hoskote, ibid., p. 37)
Tyeb’s handling of the medium, and choice of composition, allow the two figures to exist individually and as a unit, both in form and spirit. For some artists a specific image becomes the leitmotiv in their body of work. In the paintings of Sayed Haider Raza this is certainly true. The bindu dominates his canvases, to become the primordial symbol which takes on multiple meanings in his evolution over seventy years of painting.
Gestation dates from 1989, and was conceived in the most significant decade of his work when Raza was developing the essential vocabulary used in his paintings. The Bindu, the circle, contains in its form a sense of completeness or ‘wholeness’. It is used here as a circle within a square, a mandala, which is the universal symbol of sacred geography. Quite possibly, as used in paintings such as that powerful image titled Ma (1981), the bindu becomes for Raza the embodiment of India, his motherland.

‘In the formative years in the late forties and fifties Mehta’s work was typified by a direct rendering of experience on the surface. His paintings in sombre tones could loosely be termed expressionistic and articulated the fate of individuals who were in some way cornered by fate. The thickly stroked paint would layer the surface with a heavy patina of disquiet. The rendering of colours, of equal tonality and applied in verisimilitude, provided a cohesion, which would yet seem like a fierce interlocking. A compressed battle would ensue also between the figure and the space surrounding it, interpenetrative as two entities, which would coalesce to form an independent relationship creating a new interpretative reality.’ (Yashodhara Dalmia, Tyeb Mehta Triumph of Vision, exhibition catalogue, Vadehra Art Gallery, New Delhi, 2011, p. 5)
Across the several decades of Tyeb Mehta’s career, from the early gestural strokes and thick impasto surfaces, to the later knife-edge, flat planes of colour, the subjects of his paintings have always remained selective and unwavering. Deeply embedded within his choice of subjects, were the twin experiences of violence and trauma that remained consistent, sometimes unbridled and lashing; other times bubbling dangerously beneath the surface of the canvas. The trussed bull and the rickshaw were two of the earliest images he explored, and would subsequently appear in various manifestations throughout his career; linked together, occasionally, by the mentally resilient but physically fractured human figure, that always remained the fulcrum of his practice. The current work from 1962, painted during his second stay in London, is probably one of the earliest examples where the trussed bull and the rickshaw have come together in one image; t

provenance_text:
Formerly in the collection of the artist M.F. Husain.
The current painting was originally part of M.F. Husain’s personal collection. The two artists shared a special bond, having met when they both lived in Indore. After moving from Pandharpur at a young age, Husain would often sit at his uncle Murad’s place of business, contributing daily through both a stack of sketches, alongside his flair for accurate accounting. It was on one of these days in the bazaar that he ran into N.S. Bendre, who was in the midst of painting en plein air. So enamoured was he with Bendre’s technique, that from then on, Husain would ride his bicycle into the countryside with Bendre to paint the landscape, as opposed to the solitary journeys he was used to up until then. Bendre, who was then teaching at the Indore School of Art, was quick to recognise the burgeoning talent of a young artist, and suggested to Husain’s father that his son consider studying painting seriously. Husain, though, was not very keen to spend so many years being taught what, in fact, came instinctively to him, and opted out of a formal art education, a fact that set him apart within his generation of artists. His creativity stemmed from the life experiences he witnessed and was immersed in, rather than what he was taught in a more structured setting. Nonetheless, Husain’s meeting with Bendre was instrumental in convincing his father to allow him to pursue his dream of painting.
Husain moved to Mumbai shortly after in 1941, and would live there for the majority of the following decades, making it one of his primary homes. Bendre moved to Mumbai in 1966, once he retired from the Faculty of Fine Arts in Vadodara. Even though their artistic practices and careers unfolded very differently in the interim years, Husain ensured that he visited Bendre regularly at his home in Kalanagar to pay his respects to his mentor; a favourite stop on his way to or from the airport. Furthermore, in 1988, Husain, created a painting at a l

title: Acrylic on canvas
literature_text:
P. Bhargav, Paintings by M.F. Husain, Mumbai, 2004, unpaginated, illustrated.
P. Bhargav, Vision XX Century: Paintings by M. F. Husain, Mumbai, 2004, unpaginated, illustrated.
K. Bikram Singh, Maqbool Fida Husain, New Delhi, 2007, p. 157, illustrated.
In this mysterious painting, Husain brings us two exquisite figures: one ethereal, headless, swirling in motion; the other graceful, one hand shaping a mudra and the other cradling Ganesha, the god of auspicious beginnings. Clearly these figures are to be read as two forms of the Devi or Mother Goddess: one attuned to the ceaseless momentum of the universe, the other anchored to the felicity of the home, each an aspect of a larger and dynamic whole. They bear effective witness to the axial principles of his art: the crisp linearity, the compression of cultural detail into elegant figuration, the choreographic energy of the figures, and the expressiveness conveyed through brevity of gesture. Husain carries forward, in this 2004 painting, his 1950s preoccupation with India’s robust folk and elegant classical traditions. India, in Husain’s world-view, was a complex palimpsest of cultures, religions, philosophies, and ways of life, all converging and forming a composite tapestry through constant mutual engagement. Husain was a product of Nehruvian India; his imagination was liberal and expansive. While remaining committed to his own Islamic faith, he opened himself to the importance of other paths of inquiry and devotion, presenting each one as part of a universal history of the spiritual quest. [RH]
exhibited_text: M. F. Husain: An Artist’s Vision of the XX Century, Burj Al Arab, Dubai, 16 June 2004.

This painting carries an intriguing visual appeal. While the Communist banner, with its gold star on a red field, is the leitmotif of the composition, the entire painting appears as though it were painted on the folds of a rippling banner, blue and red. The strong vertical emphasis is provided by a figure almost certainly based on a photograph or several photographs. Might this be Lenin proclaiming the Bolshevik Revolution in 1917? Is there an element of Chairman Mao to this figure, although without the famous Mao jacket? Is Trotsky implied? While the precise identification of its protagonist remains open, this painting reminds us of one of the most powerful political movements of the 20th century: Communism, which transformed Russia and China and held out the hope of social justice and political equity to innumerable societies in the Global South. Unfortunately, these utopian ideals were overshadowed in practice by the most brutal forms of coercion, control and oppression, so that the egalitarian paradise promised by Communism was more often actualised as a vast open-air prison. [RH]

The supple, athletic protagonist of this painting suggests the celebrated dancer and choreographer Merce Cunningham and the legendary mime artist Marcel Marceau. This painting could be seen in continuity with the perennial series of works in which Husain expressed his admiration for the grace, tempo and visceral energy of the performing arts. Lithe and agile, this dancing figure evokes the world of modernist dance – and yet the gesture of the hand and the presence of the bird recall the mudras of classical Indian dance and the conventions of the raga-mala and barahmasa painting cycles from the Rajput and Pahari ateliers. [RH]

title: Acrylic on canvas
literature_text:
P. Bhargav, Paintings by M.F. Husain, Mumbai, 2004, unpaginated, illustrated.
P. Bhargav, Vision XX Century: Paintings by M. F. Husain, Mumbai, 2004, unpaginated, illustrated.
Empire, war and revolution are the leitmotifs of this painting. The upper left quarter of the painting is dominated by a figure drawn from the annals of Orientalist lore: a Chinese emperor or mandarin. At the lower left is T.E. Lawrence, the classical scholar, archaeologist and guerrilla warrior better known as Lawrence of Arabia. The figure in green, sporting a high hat adorned with the Turkish crescent moon and star,  may well be a composite of the epochal Turkish revolutionary Kemal Pasha, the great Turkish Communist poet Nazim Hikmet and the splendid Egyptian-born actor Omar Sharif playing Dr Zhivago in the eponymous film by David Lean. It is fascinating to map the currents of Husain’s imagination – here, he is clearly revisiting the entangled histories of the Ottoman Empire and its successor states, as well as the Soviet Union and China, thinking through the historic transition from empires to modern, militarist nation-states. [RH]
exhibited_text:
M. F. Husain, Pierre Cardin Centre, Paris, November 2004.

A tribute to the genre of film noir, which Husain loved, this painting is based on a film still showing the legendary actor Humphrey Bogart leaning against a lamp-post, in his trademark trench coat and broad-brimmed hat pulled down over his eyes, seemingly slouching at ease yet keeping the street and all its denizens under watchful scrutiny. The detective, the criminal and the spy all emerge from these fictional and cinematic narratives as complex figures, driven by mixed motives and fluctuating commitments, shifting moral gear midway through the actions they undertake: operators in a grey area of the Cold War and the general climate of uncertainty and precariousness left behind in the wake of World War II. [RH]
exhibited_text: M. F. Husain: An Artist’s Vision of the XX Century, Burj Al Arab, Dubai, 16 June 2004.

A send-up of Empire and its fantasies, this painting modelled largely in grey, cream and ivory features a bestial figure costumed in the robes of imperial domination, troops pulling artillery into position, an agitated horse emitting a whinny, and a naked woman holding on to a red banner that is, so to speak, off-stage. The beast of Empire has seemingly stepped out of a heraldic coat of arms and assumed visceral reality. The woman is Freedom, perhaps, reaching out for the banner of Revolution. As always, the authority of the State – trapped in its own pathologies of insecurity and paranoia – can only roll out the cannons when faced with profound philosophical doubts or questions as to the justice of its cause. [RH]

P. Bhargav, Vision XX Century: Paintings by M. F. Husain, Mumbai, 2004, unpaginated, illustrated.
Presumably Husain’s thoughts had circled back to the lore of dogfights and trench warfare that form the staple of World War I stories. Here, we may read of the harsh transition from an age of chivalry in which the cavalry still rode out to war to an age of annihilation when mustard gas overwhelmed the infantry trapped in trenches and grenades struck terror at a distance. The red biplane that hangs over this composition may perhaps be the chosen craft of the Red Baron, the German fighter ace Manfred Freiherr von Richthofen. Two soldiers seated at a rather Parisian cafe table below appear to be raising a toast to their aerial colleague. Are they celebrating war or are they honouring the return of peace? [RH]
exhibited_text: M. F. Husain: An Artist’s Vision of the XX Century, Burj Al Arab, Dubai, 16 June 2004.

The record turning on the plate of the gramophone, and the central figure of the dog, remind us of the once well-loved household brand of HMV, ‘His Master’s Voice’, who led the market in recorded music. The figure of the woman is not easy to identify. Is she the figure of vengeance and harsh justice known to posterity as the Bandit Queen? Or is she a folk dancer, observed on the side of a highway somewhere in semi-rural India by the artist? Or does she represent the momentous transition in the world of India’s female musicians, from the brocade-and-cutglass domain of the tawaif to the modern stage and screen? [RH]

Little goblin figures, evidently cousins to the more benign cherubs of Christian art, hold up a bench in this triptych. An unlikely cast of characters is perched on this bench: from left to right, an athlete holding an Olympic torch; two figures, one wearing the shirt and braces of a European explorer, the other a native of precolonial Africa, evidently if startlingly in companionable conversation; and another pair, one in Arab robes and headgear, the other in colonial jacket and sola topi, puffing at a pipe. Related in spirit and figuration to Husain’s Raj series, this set of panels appears to gesture towards the ideal of peaceful dialogue and coexistence across the asymmetries imposed by a brutal history of colonialism and empire. [RH]

K. Bikram Singh, Maqbool Fida Husain, New Delhi, 2007, p. 28, illustrated.
Mahatma Gandhi was a perennial source of inspiration to Husain: the man who led India to freedom, the politician who became a saint, the maestro of non-violence, the pacifist who stood his ground against the guns of the British Empire. This portrait of Gandhi is headless; instead of a head, the Mahatma’s face is plunged in shadow, with a dove, that bird of Eternal Peace, hovering in front of it. The crisp pleats of Gandhi’s dhoti, his famous pocket watch attached to his waistband, and his customary staff – Husain commemorates Gandhi through the iconography that the great man created for himself. In the lower right-hand corner of the composition, we see the archetypal figure of the Indian peasant – for whose rights the Mahatma toiled and fought – with his plough slung over his shoulder. [RH]
exhibited_text: M. F. Husain: An Artist’s Vision of the XX Century, Burj Al Arab, Dubai, 16 June 2004.

K. Bikram Singh, Maqbool Fida Husain, New Delhi, 2007, p. 324, illustrated.
Charlie Chaplin was a reigning icon of the 20th century. In film after film, whether ‘The Kid’ or ‘The Gold Rush’ or ‘City Lights’ or ‘Modern Times’, he played the marginal figure of the tramp or the temporary worker, Everyman at the edge of a world of profit and consumption that excludes him. It now seems clear that Chaplin drew on his Roma heritage to create this figure of the peripheral person whose humour helps him to survive in a cruel world and to create an ephemeral order of being in which he may flourish on the basis of fantasy until he is brought down to earth again, still claiming our emotional allegiance. Here, Husain presents Chaplin with one of the child actors who featured in some of his best-known films; they watch as a rocket is launched into space, going past them in a burst of fire and speed. Discreetly, Husain sets up a counterpoint between social and economic deficit on the one hand, and vast expenditure on State programs on the other. Who may dare to dream, and whose dreams will come true, the artist seems to ask us. [RH]
exhibited_text: M. F. Husain: An Artist’s Vision of the XX Century, Burj Al Arab, Dubai, 16 June 2004.

K. Bikram Singh, Maqbool Fida Husain, New Delhi, 2007, p. 159, illustrated.
The graceful figure of the dancer at the heart of this composition may well have been drawn, by Husain, from the acclaimed modern dancer, choreographer and filmmaker Uday Shankar’s film opera ‘Kalpana’ (1948). The theme of tradition coming into contact with modernity is given form through the adjacency of mudra and machine. How might the language of temple sculptures be piloted through the metropolis? The figure is based on the Chola bronze of Shiva as Nataraja, the Lord of the Dance, engaged in the tandava, his cosmic dance of creation and destruction. However, this figure seems far too gentle for such a colossal mandate.
We may also sense intimations of a childhood world in the colour wheel and petals, the hint of an animal exiting the picture frame at lower right. We are reminded that, as a child, Husain’s imagination was nourished by the fantasy world of handmade toys, the rhythms of percussive music, folk song, and a culture shaped around everyday rituals. Alongside working as a billboard painter for the popular Hindi cinema of Bombay during the 1940s, Husain was also a designer for a famous manufacturer of toys and children’s furniture in Bombay, Fantasie. Given its elite clientele, Fantasie tended towards European fairtyales and fables, and a more Western figuration. By contrast, Husain pioneered toys, dolls, narratives and figuration that drew on Indian models, in consonance with the Jataka and Panchatantra literature and the Ajanta murals from India’s Buddhist past. [RH]
exhibited_text: M. F. Husain: An Artist’s Vision of the XX Century, Burj Al Arab, Dubai, 16 June 2004.

Husain drew on his childhood memories of Indore, a princely state associated in vassalage with the British Raj, for the figures he depicted in his Raj series. Closely akin to that dramatis personae is this pair of women, one Indian and wearing a sari, the other Western, white, wearing a summer dress and a hat. This form of twinning recurred in Husain’s art, and may reflect his belief in the power of human solidarity to overcome the artificial yet powerful constructs of political, social, cultural and racial hierarchy. [RH]

The Yalta Conference took place in Crimea between 4 and 11 February 1945, before World War II had come to an end, both on the Western and Eastern fronts. It was a meeting between the leaders of the alliance that had come together to oppose the ascendancy of Nazi Germany and militarist Japan. The key players at the Yalta Conference were Franklin D. Roosevelt, President of the USA, Winston Churchill, Prime Minister of the UK, and Joseph Stalin, the dictator who presided over the fortunes of the Soviet Union. The agenda was to discuss how Nazi Germany and militarist Japan were to be overwhelmed in a final series of offensives, and how Europe was to be reorganised after the fall of Nazi Germany and the end of its occupation of a large number of European countries. Husain approaches his protagonists in a near-caricatural spirit, modelling his treatment on the visualities of the movie poster, the comic strip and the propaganda pamphlet. The triad of world leaders are shown manipulating hapless people around a chessboard, cynical and pragmatic in their assessments of the situation. [RH]
exhibited_text: M. F. Husain: An Artist’s Vision of the XX Century, Burj Al Arab, Dubai, 16 June 2004.

The Tireless Chronicler of a Dramatic Century:
M F Husain’s OPCE Series (2004)
Ranjit Hoskote
I.
A foundational figure in the history of modern Indian art, Maqbool Fida Husain (1913-2011) was a dominant presence on the Indian cultural scene for more than six decades. Together with his friends Francis Newton Souza, Sayed Haider Raza, Krishnaji Howlaji Ara, Sadanand Bakre and H.A. Gade, he was a founding member of the Progressive Artists Group, formed with a buoyant yet pensive optimism in Bombay (now Mumbai) in 1947. These young artists meant to assert their defiance of the prevailing norms of Western-style academic realism in India; they also intended to counter the nostalgic tendency to adapt the style of the pre-colonial miniaturist ateliers. In the event, they learned to look outwards to the avant gardes of Europe and the USA while also gleaning inspiration from the heritage of Maurya, Shunga and Gupta sculpture and the paintings of the Rajput and Pahari schools.
Among these artists, it was Husain who was notably adept at a wide variety of media. His practice ranged from oil painting, acrylics and watercolour, through lithography and serigraphy, to tapestries and textiles, sculpture, architecture and, late in life, the installation form. Husain’s was a plural and prodigiously expansive creative imagination: he was also a film-maker and a photographer, a multilingual poet and memoirist who wrote in Urdu, Hindi and English. When he died in London at the age of 98, in June 2011, he had traced a biographical arc through one of the most cataclysmic yet transformative centuries in human history. He had lived through two World Wars, the collapse of the Ottoman and Russian empires and the rise of the Kemalist and the Bolshe

An essay in the genre of the still life or a stage set with props – regard it as you will – this painting highlights some of the key texts that influenced politics, culture and the understanding of the human mind’s complexities during the 20th century. On and below the chair that occupies most of the picture space in this painting, we find Karl Marx’s Das Kapital, which set off a series of revolutions; Rabindranath Tagore’s Gitanjali, which helped its author win the Nobel Prize; T.S. Eliot’s The Waste Land, which announced the arrival of a modernism that was montaged together from the kaleidoscopic diversity and dissonance of modern urban experience; and Carl Gustaf Jung’s works, his explorations of the individual and collective psyche, with its drives, archetypes, crises and processes of resolution. Significantly, a hunter or explorer’s pith hat is hung on the chair, a rifle rests against its back, and a boot is poised beneath it. Husain appears to be bringing together the via contemplative, the interior life of the mind, and the via active, the exterior life of action – while also gesturing towards the intimate and inevitable connection between the intellectual pursuits of the scholar and poet and the violence of the hunter, the soldier, the explorer and exploiter of the natural world. [RH]

M. F. Husain, Pierre Cardin Centre, Paris, November 2004.

P. Bhargav, Vision XX Century: Paintings by M. F. Husain, Mumbai, 2004, unpaginated, illustrated.
This triptych is arguably the most enigmatic of the paintings in the OPCE series. We are literally forced to ask: What is going on here? A group of cherubs holds up a bench – this was a recurrent tableau in Husain’s late work, the frontal or rear view of figures seated at a bench or table precariously held up by magical helper figures. Two explorers or soldiers sit at one end of the composition, while a Jeevesian figure in an incongruous top hat and tailcoat brings in a drink on a tray at the other end. But these are not the details that captivate our attention.
Our gaze is immediately seized by the strange figure who lies on the bench, oddly occupying most of the central panel, with his legs draped over the lap of one of the explorers or soldiers. His head is laid on a pillow closer to the butleresque figure. The only figure to which this prone figure bears any resemblance is that of Christ, as traditionally depicted in a Deposition scene, when his body is taken down from the Cross and laid out reverently. This moment of grief accounts for one of the abiding themes of Gothic, Renaissance and Baroque art. However, there is no Christ-like dignity or reverence attendant upon the figure in this painting, though.
If anything, Husain’s painting conveys a sly, cheeky humour. So perhaps the prone figure is simply a colleague of the gentlemen seated insouciantly at far left, doubtless struck down by sunstroke? Or an athlete seeking repose, perhaps or one of the spirited runners from the screen classic, ‘Chariots of Fire’? We shall never know. But we can go on guessing, for Maqbool Fida Husain continues to pose us riddles long after he has passed into Eternity

provenance_text:
Sarala Art Centre, Chennai.
Laxma Goud remains one of India’s most versatile artists, moving effortlessly between pencil, ink, watercolour, terracotta, bronze and printmaking. At the heart of all his artistic endeavours is his incredible skill as a draughtsman, articulated in all his works with a searing honesty visible in every fine detail.
His sketchbooks were extremely important to him, and he would have one as his constant companion, allowing him to create drawings instantly, on any of his travels or meanders through various cities. It is a habit he learned from his teacher, N.S. Bendre, during his years studying at the Faculty of Fine Arts in Vadodara. Bendre was also known to always have the tools of his trade handy with him, as he often sketched impromptu en plein air, while visiting various Indian villages and farmlands.
While in art school, these sketchbooks also allowed for new learnings. Often, K.G. Subramanyan, his teacher and guide, would himself sketch in Laxma’s sketchbooks, teaching him meticulously about technique and method. In fact, it was Subramanyan, upon seeing Laxma’s raw talent in his drawings, who instilled in the young artist, that drawing too was an art, and should not be dismissed as many tended to do in these early years. Laxma admits, that even though he is equally comfortable working in ink or watercolour, he prefers the language of drawing for what it allows him to capture, and for the sheer process itself.
The current sketchbook was used by the artist in the 1980s and contains eighteen sketches executed either in 1984 or 1986.
